• Step 1: Calculate Wells Score.
• If Likely (> 4): Next best step is CTPA (Imaging).
• If Unlikely (≤ 4): Next best step is D-Dimer.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient is Unstable (Hypotensive), the next best step is Bedside Echo or Empiric Heparin (if no contraindications) before any imaging that requires moving the patient.

quiz.q: What is the next best step for a stable patient with a high probability (Wells > 4) of PE?

title: 🚑 BLUNT ABDOMINAL TRAUMA
content:
Managing a patient after a motor vehicle accident (MVA).

• If Unstable (Hypotensive): Next best step is FAST Exam (Ultrasound at bedside).
• If Stable: Next best step is CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If the FAST is Positive and the patient is Unstable, the next best step is Immediate Laparotomy (OR). Do not send an unstable patient to the CT suite!

quiz.q: What is the next best step for a hemodynamically stable patient with blunt abdominal trauma?

title: 🎀 BREAST MASS WORKUP (AGE-BASED)
content:
The investigation depends strictly on age.

• Age < 30: Next best step is Ultrasound (dense breast tissue makes mammogram useless).
• Age ≥ 30: Next best step is Mammogram (+/- Ultrasound).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a cyst is found and aspirated, and the fluid is Bloody or the mass doesn't disappear, the next best step is Biopsy (Core biopsy is preferred over FNA).

quiz.q: What is the initial imaging of choice for a breast mass in a 24-year-old woman?

title: 🤰 AMENORRHEA: THE FIRST STEP
content:
A patient presents with missed periods.

• Absolute First Step: Urine β-hCG (Pregnancy test). Even if she says she's not active!
• Next Steps (if negative): TSH, Prolactin, and FSH levels.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If secondary amenorrhea is confirmed and labs are normal, the next best step is the Progesterone Challenge Test to assess estrogen status and outflow tract.

quiz.q: What is the first-line investigation for any woman of reproductive age with amenorrhea?

title: 🚽 ACUTE SCROTUM: TORSION?
content:
• Step 1: Clinical Exam (Absent cremasteric reflex, Prehn's sign negative).
• If High Suspicion: Next best step is Immediate Surgical Exploration.
• If Low/Equivocal Suspicion: Next best step is Doppler Ultrasound.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT delay surgery to get an ultrasound if the clinical picture is classic (sudden pain, high-riding testis). Time is Testicle! (6-hour window).

quiz.q: In a classic case of suspected testicular torsion, what is the next best step?

title: 🩸 GI BLEED: STABILIZE FIRST
content:
Patient vomiting blood or passing melena.

• Step 1: ABCs and Resuscitation (Two large-bore IVs, IV fluids).
• Step 2: NG Tube lavage (to confirm upper vs lower).
• Step 3: Endoscopy (within 24 hours).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient is hypotensive, the next best step is never the endoscopy suite. You must Resuscitate first. Stable = Scope; Unstable = IV Fluids/Blood.

quiz.q: What is the priority in a patient presenting with massive hematemesis and a blood pressure of 80/40?

title: 👶 FEBRILE INFANT (< 28 DAYS)
content:
A neonate with a temperature ≥ 38.0°C.

• Next Best Step: Full Sepsis Workup + Admission.
• Workup includes: CBC, Blood cultures, Urinalysis/Culture, and Lumbar Puncture.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Even if the baby looks 'perfectly fine', a fever in a neonate (< 28 days) is an automatic admission for IV Antibiotics (Ampicillin + Cefotaxime/Gentamicin) until cultures are negative.

quiz.q: Does a 20-day-old infant with a fever of 38.2°C require a lumbar puncture?
quiz.a: Yes, as part of a full sepsis workup.

title: 🧠 HEAD TRAUMA: CANADIAN CT RULES
content:
Deciding who needs a CT Head after minor injury (GCS 13-15).

CT is required if:
1. GCS < 15 at 2 hours post-injury.
2. Suspected open/depressed skull fracture.
3. Any sign of basal skull fracture (Racoon eyes, Battle sign).
4. Vomiting ≥ 2 times.
5. Age ≥ 65 years.

⚠️ Exam Trap: These rules only apply if there was a loss of consciousness, amnesia, or confusion. If the patient is ≥ 65, they get a CT automatically!

quiz.q: A 70-year-old man falls and hits his head with brief loss of consciousness. His GCS is 15. Next best step?

title: 🫀 CHEST PAIN: THE 10-MINUTE RULE
content:
Patient arrives at ED with retrosternal chest pain.

• First Best Step: ECG (must be performed and interpreted within 10 minutes).
• Simultaneously: Aspirin (chewed) + Nitroglycerin (if not contraindicated).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT wait for Troponin results to treat a STEMI. If the ECG shows ST elevation, the next best step is Activation of the Cath Lab (PCI).

quiz.q: What is the goal time for obtaining an ECG in a patient with suspected ACS?

title: 🌬️ DYSPHAGIA: SOLIDS VS LIQUIDS
content:
The 'Next Step' depends on the type of dysphagia.

• Solids only (Mechanical): Next best step is Endoscopy (rule out cancer/stricture).
• Solids + Liquids (Motility): Next best step is Barium Swallow or Manometry.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has a history of Heartburn (GERD) and now has solid-food dysphagia, think Peptic Stricture. If they have weight loss/smoking, think Adenocarcinoma.

quiz.q: What is the best initial test for a patient with dysphagia to both solids and liquids?

title: ⚖️ SUICIDE RISK ASSESSMENT
content:
A patient expresses hopelessness.

• Next Best Step: Direct Questioning about suicidal ideation, intent, and plan.
• Next (if high risk): Ensure Safety (Admission, 1:1 observation, remove lethal means).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Asking about suicide does NOT increase the risk of the patient doing it. It is the only way to assess safety and is the most important step in the interview.

quiz.q: What is the first thing you should do if a patient mentions they 'can't go on'?
quiz.a: Ask directly about suicidal thoughts and plans.

title: 🦴 BACK PAIN: RED FLAGS
content:
Most back pain is mechanical (reassurance/NSAIDs).

• Red Flags: Fever, weight loss, history of cancer, Cauda Equina signs (saddle anesthesia, bowel/bladder issues).
• If Red Flags present: Next best step is MRI Spine (Emergent if Cauda Equina).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In the absence of red flags, the next best step is not imaging. It is NSAIDs and encouraging activity. Do not order X-rays for routine back pain!

quiz.q: What is the imaging of choice for suspected spinal cord compression?

title: 🦠 NEEDLESTICK INJURY
content:
Healthcare worker stuck with a used needle.

• Absolute First Step: Wash the area with soap and water.
• Next Best Step: Assess the source patient's status and start PEP (Post-exposure prophylaxis) if indicated (ideally < 2 hours).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do not wait for lab results from the source patient to start PEP if the risk is high. You can stop PEP later if the results come back negative.

quiz.q: What is the very first action after a needlestick exposure?
quiz.a: Wash the wound with soap and water.

title: 🧪 DVT: THE D-DIMER TRAP
content:
Suspected leg swelling.

• Wells Score High: Next best step is Compression Ultrasound (skip D-dimer).
• Wells Score Low: Next best step is D-Dimer.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Remember that D-Dimer is only useful for its Negative Predictive Value (to rule out). If it's positive, you still need the ultrasound. If the Wells score is high, a negative D-dimer is not enough to rule out DVT.

quiz.q: For a patient with a high clinical probability of DVT, what is the next best step?

title: 🧂 HYPERCALCEMIA: INITIAL TX
content:
Calcium > 3.0 mmol/L.

• Next Best Step: Aggressive IV Fluids (Normal Saline).
• Why? These patients are severely dehydrated due to polyuria.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do not give Loop Diuretics (Furosemide) unless the patient is fluid-overloaded. The priority is hydration. Bisphosphonates take 48-72 hours to work and are not the 'first' step in an acute crisis.

quiz.q: What is the most important initial management for severe hypercalcemia?

title: ⚡ AORTIC DISSECTION
content:
Tearing chest pain radiating to the back (between scapulae).

• Next Best Step: CT Angiography (if stable) or TEE (if unstable).
• Management:
- Type A (Ascending): Surgery (Emergency).
- Type B (Descending): Medical (BP control with Beta-blockers - Labetalol).

⚠️ Exam Trap: You MUST lower the Heart Rate and Blood Pressure simultaneously to reduce 'shear stress'. Target SBP 100-120 mmHg.

quiz.q: What is the first-line medical management for a Type B aortic dissection?

title: 🧪 METABOLIC ACIDOSIS (ANION GAP)
content:
Calculating the cause of low bicarbonate.

$$AG = Na^+ - (Cl^- + HCO_3^-)$$
• Normal AG: 8 - 12 mmol/L.
• High AG (MUDPILES): Methanol, Uremia, DKA, Propylene glycol, Iron/Isoniazid, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the AG is high, always calculate the Osmolar Gap to look for toxic alcohols (Methanol/Ethylene glycol). If AG is normal, the cause is usually Diarrhea or RTA.

quiz.q: What is the formula for Anion Gap?

title: 🦵 COMPARTMENT SYNDROME
content:
Surgical Emergency! Increased pressure within a muscle compartment.

• Signs: Pain out of proportion to injury, pain with passive stretch (most sensitive), paresthesia, pulselessness (late).
• Dx: Clinical suspicion; can confirm with compartment pressure (> 30 mmHg).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT wait for pulselessness to diagnose; if the leg is rock-hard and the patient is screaming in pain despite morphine, the next best step is Immediate Fasciotomy.

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive early clinical sign of compartment syndrome?
quiz.a: Pain with passive stretching of the affected muscles.

title: 👶 FIFTH DISEASE (PARVOVIRUS B19)
content:
• Signs: 'Slapped-cheek' rash followed by a lacy, reticular rash on the body.
• Risk: Aplastic crisis in Sickle Cell; Hydrops Fetalis in pregnant women.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Once the rash appears, the child is no longer contagious and can return to school. The danger is to pregnant women who are exposed before the rash develops.

quiz.q: Which virus causes Fifth Disease?

title: ⚡ FEBRILE SEIZURES
content:
Seizures triggered by fever in kids (6 months to 5 years).

• Simple: Generalized, < 15 mins, only one in 24h. (Tx: Reassurance).
• Complex: Focal, > 15 mins, or multiple in 24h. (Tx: Investigate).

⚠️ Exam Trap: The most important step is to Find the source of the fever. Do NOT perform an EEG or Neuroimaging for a simple febrile seizure. Use Acetaminophen for comfort, but it does NOT prevent the next seizure.

quiz.q: What defines a 'simple' febrile seizure?
quiz.a: Generalized, lasting less than 15 minutes, and occurring once in 24 hours.

title: 🎗️ POST-MENOPAUSAL BLEEDING (PMB)
content:
Cancer until proven otherwise!

• Next Best Step: Transvaginal Ultrasound (TVUS).
• If Endometrial Strip > 4mm: Next best step is Endometrial Biopsy.
• If < 4mm: Observation (likely atrophy).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Even if the patient is on HRT or has a known cause (atrophy), you MUST rule out Endometrial Cancer first.

quiz.q: What is the cut-off thickness on ultrasound for an endometrial biopsy in PMB?

title: 🤰 NST INTERPRETATION
content:
Non-Stress Test for fetal well-being.

• Reactive (Normal): 2+ accelerations (15 bpm for 15 sec) in 20 mins.
• Non-reactive: Next best step is to stimulate the fetus (Vibroacoustic) or extend the test to 40 mins.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the NST remains non-reactive after 40 minutes, the next best step is a Biophysical Profile (BPP) or a Contraction Stress Test.

quiz.q: What is the next step after a non-reactive NST despite stimulation?

title: 🧪 SPONTANEOUS BACTERIAL PERITONITIS
content:
Infection of ascitic fluid in cirrhosis.

• Signs: Fever, altered mental status, abdominal pain.
• Dx: Paracentesis showing PMNs > 250 cells/mm³.
• Tx: 3rd gen Cephalosporin (Cefotaxime) + Albumin.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Prophylaxis: If the patient has had SBP before or has low-protein ascites, they need lifelong prophylaxis with Ciprofloxacin or Septra.

quiz.q: What is the diagnostic threshold for PMN count in ascitic fluid for SBP?

title: 🦋 PHEOCHROMOCYTOMA
content:
Catecholamine-secreting tumor of the adrenal medulla.

• Triad: Episodic Headache, Sweating, and Palpitations.
• Screening: 24-hr Urinary Metanephrines or Plasma fractionated metanephrines.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Pre-operative prep is CRITICAL: Give Alpha-blocker (Phenoxybenzamine) FIRST, then Beta-blockers. If you give Beta-blockers first, the 'unopposed alpha' will cause a fatal hypertensive crisis.

quiz.q: In what order should medications be given before pheochromocytoma surgery?

title: 🧠 SEROTONIN SYNDROME VS NMS
content:
FeatureSerotonin SyndromeNMS
CauseSSRIs / MAOIsAntipsychotics
ReflexesHyperreflexia / ClonusHyporeflexia / Rigidity
OnsetSudden (< 24h)Gradual (Days)

⚠️ Exam Trap: Lead-pipe rigidity is the hallmark of NMS. Clonus (shaking limbs) is the hallmark of Serotonin Syndrome. Both are medical emergencies requiring drug discontinuation and cooling.

quiz.q: Which syndrome presents with hyperreflexia and neuromuscular clonus?

title: 👁️ CHEMICAL EYE BURNS
content:
Ocular Emergency! Acid or Alkali splash.

• Absolute First Step: Immediate Irrigation with saline or water for at least 30 mins (until pH is 7.0 - 7.2).
• Note: Alkali (bleach, drain cleaner) is worse because it causes liquefactive necrosis and deep penetration.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT wait to check vision (Acuity) or call a specialist. Irrigate first, then assess the damage. This is one of the few times acuity is NOT the first step in eye cases.

quiz.q: What is the priority management for a chemical burn to the eye?

title: 🧪 CARBON MONOXIDE (CO) POISONING
content:
The 'Silent Killer'.

• Signs: Headache, dizziness, nausea, 'Cherry-red' skin (late/rare).
• Labs: Normal SpO2 (pulse ox is fake!), high Carboxyhemoglobin level.
• Tx: 100% Hyperbaric Oxygen (if severe/pregnant).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Pulse oximetry cannot distinguish between HgbO2 and HgbCO. A patient can have a Normal SpO2 and still be dying from CO poisoning. Check an ABG for carboxyhemoglobin!

quiz.q: What is the specific test to diagnose carbon monoxide poisoning?
quiz.a: Co-oximetry (Carboxyhemoglobin level) via ABG or VBG.

title: 🧴 SCC VS BCC VS MELANOMA
content:
FeatureBasal Cell (BCC)Squamous Cell (SCC)Melanoma
LookPearly / TelangiectasiaScaly / CrustedPigmented / ABCDE
RiskSun exposureSun / ImmunosuppressionSun / Family history

⚠️ Exam Trap: SCC can arise from Actinic Keratosis and is more aggressive than BCC. In a transplant patient, SCC is the most common and dangerous skin cancer.

quiz.q: Which skin cancer is most commonly associated with immunosuppression?

title: 🩸 SMEAR CHALLENGE: SCHISTOCYTES
content:
• Significance: Suggests MAHA (Microangiopathic Hemolytic Anemia).
• Differential: TTP, HUS, DIC, or Mechanical valves.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If you see Schistocytes + Fever + Confusion + Low Platelets, the next best step is Emergent Plasmapheresis (TTP). Do not wait for ADAMTS13 levels!

quiz.q: What do schistocytes on a peripheral smear indicate?

title: ⚖️ ELDER ABUSE REPORTING
content:
A sensitive Canadian legal requirement.

• Rule: Unlike child abuse (which is mandatory everywhere), reporting elder abuse to authorities varies by province, but investigating and protecting the patient is always mandatory.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If an elderly patient with Capacity refuses to let you report abuse, you must respect their Autonomy (unless it's in a long-term care home, where reporting is usually mandatory). Always offer resources/social work.

quiz.q: Is reporting elder abuse always legally mandatory if the patient has full capacity?
quiz.a: No (depends on province/setting), but ensuring safety is mandatory.

title: 🍔 AAA SCREENING (CANADA)
content:
Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm screening guidelines.

• Who: Men aged 65 - 80 (One-time screen).
• How: Abdominal Ultrasound.
• Management:
- < 5.0 cm: Follow with serial U/S.
- > 5.5 cm: Refer for Repair (EVAR or Open).

⚠️ Exam Trap: For Women, there is no routine screening recommendation unless they have high-risk factors (Smoking/Family Hx).

quiz.q: What is the threshold size for recommending surgical repair of an AAA in a man?

title: 🧪 CKD MANAGEMENT
content:
Chronic Kidney Disease: Slowing progression.

• Target BP: < 130/80 mmHg.
• First-line Med: ACEi or ARB (especially if albuminuria present).
• Complications: Anemia (Low EPO), Bone disease (High Phos, Low Vit D).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In CKD, the kidneys stop activating Vitamin D and stop excreting Phosphorus. This leads to Secondary Hyperparathyroidism. Treatment is Phosphate binders and Calcitriol.

quiz.q: Which class of antihypertensives is renal-protective in patients with CKD and proteinuria?

title: 🦠 TUBERCULOSIS (PPD / IGRA)
content:
Screening for latent TB.

• Positive Test: Induration ≥ 10mm (average risk) or ≥ 5mm (high risk/HIV).
• Next Best Step: Chest X-ray to rule out active disease.
• If CXR negative: Treat for Latent TB (Isoniazid for 9 months).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has had the BCG vaccine, the PPD might be false positive. In this case, use IGRA (QuantiFERON) as it is not affected by BCG.

quiz.q: What is the next step for a patient with a positive PPD test?
quiz.a: Chest X-ray (to rule out active TB).

title: ⚖️ POWER OF ATTORNEY (POA)
content:
TypeResponsibility
POA for Personal CareHealth decisions, housing, food.
POA for PropertyFinances, bills, bank accounts.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A physician only consults the POA for Personal Care for medical decisions. The fact that someone has POA for Property does NOT give them the right to make health decisions!

quiz.q: Which Power of Attorney is responsible for making medical decisions for an incapable patient?
quiz.a: Power of Attorney for Personal Care.

title: 🏆 MISSION COMPLETE: MCCQE1 MINDSET
content:
Congratulations, Doctor! You now have a 500-card elite vault.

• Final Strategy:
1. Safety First: Always stabilize (ABCs) before diagnosing.
2. Autonomy: Respect the capable patient's choice, even if you disagree.
3. Canadian Rules: Think within the Canadian public health and legal framework.

🌟 THE VAULT IS CLOSED 🌟
You are ready. Trust your knowledge, stay calm, and dominate the exam. You've got this, Canada's future physician!

quiz.q: What is the physician's first priority in any trauma or emergency case?

title: ⚡ AORTIC DISSECTION
content:
Tearing chest pain radiating to the back (between scapulae).

• Next Best Step: CT Angiography (if stable) or TEE (if unstable).
• Management:
- Type A (Ascending): Surgery (Emergency).
- Type B (Descending): Medical (BP control with Beta-blockers - Labetalol).

⚠️ Exam Trap: You MUST lower the Heart Rate and Blood Pressure simultaneously to reduce 'shear stress'. Target SBP 100-120 mmHg.

quiz.q: What is the first-line medical management for a Type B aortic dissection?

title: 🧪 METABOLIC ACIDOSIS (ANION GAP)
content:
Calculating the cause of low bicarbonate.

$$AG = Na^+ - (Cl^- + HCO_3^-)$$
• Normal AG: 8 - 12 mmol/L.
• High AG (MUDPILES): Methanol, Uremia, DKA, Propylene glycol, Iron/Isoniazid, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the AG is high, always calculate the Osmolar Gap to look for toxic alcohols (Methanol/Ethylene glycol). If AG is normal, the cause is usually Diarrhea or RTA.

quiz.q: What is the formula for Anion Gap?

title: 🦵 COMPARTMENT SYNDROME
content:
Surgical Emergency! Increased pressure within a muscle compartment.

• Signs: Pain out of proportion to injury, pain with passive stretch (most sensitive), paresthesia, pulselessness (late).
• Dx: Clinical suspicion; can confirm with compartment pressure (> 30 mmHg).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT wait for pulselessness to diagnose; if the leg is rock-hard and the patient is screaming in pain despite morphine, the next best step is Immediate Fasciotomy.

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive early clinical sign of compartment syndrome?
quiz.a: Pain with passive stretching of the affected muscles.

title: 👶 FIFTH DISEASE (PARVOVIRUS B19)
content:
• Signs: 'Slapped-cheek' rash followed by a lacy, reticular rash on the body.
• Risk: Aplastic crisis in Sickle Cell; Hydrops Fetalis in pregnant women.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Once the rash appears, the child is no longer contagious and can return to school. The danger is to pregnant women who are exposed before the rash develops.

quiz.q: Which virus causes Fifth Disease?

title: ⚡ FEBRILE SEIZURES
content:
Seizures triggered by fever in kids (6 months to 5 years).

• Simple: Generalized, < 15 mins, only one in 24h. (Tx: Reassurance).
• Complex: Focal, > 15 mins, or multiple in 24h. (Tx: Investigate).

⚠️ Exam Trap: The most important step is to Find the source of the fever. Do NOT perform an EEG or Neuroimaging for a simple febrile seizure. Use Acetaminophen for comfort, but it does NOT prevent the next seizure.

quiz.q: What defines a 'simple' febrile seizure?
quiz.a: Generalized, lasting less than 15 minutes, and occurring once in 24 hours.

title: 🎗️ POST-MENOPAUSAL BLEEDING (PMB)
content:
Cancer until proven otherwise!

• Next Best Step: Transvaginal Ultrasound (TVUS).
• If Endometrial Strip > 4mm: Next best step is Endometrial Biopsy.
• If < 4mm: Observation (likely atrophy).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Even if the patient is on HRT or has a known cause (atrophy), you MUST rule out Endometrial Cancer first.

quiz.q: What is the cut-off thickness on ultrasound for an endometrial biopsy in PMB?

title: 🤰 NST INTERPRETATION
content:
Non-Stress Test for fetal well-being.

• Reactive (Normal): 2+ accelerations (15 bpm for 15 sec) in 20 mins.
• Non-reactive: Next best step is to stimulate the fetus (Vibroacoustic) or extend the test to 40 mins.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the NST remains non-reactive after 40 minutes, the next best step is a Biophysical Profile (BPP) or a Contraction Stress Test.

quiz.q: What is the next step after a non-reactive NST despite stimulation?

title: 🧪 SPONTANEOUS BACTERIAL PERITONITIS
content:
Infection of ascitic fluid in cirrhosis.

• Signs: Fever, altered mental status, abdominal pain.
• Dx: Paracentesis showing PMNs > 250 cells/mm³.
• Tx: 3rd gen Cephalosporin (Cefotaxime) + Albumin.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Prophylaxis: If the patient has had SBP before or has low-protein ascites, they need lifelong prophylaxis with Ciprofloxacin or Septra.

quiz.q: What is the diagnostic threshold for PMN count in ascitic fluid for SBP?

title: 🦋 PHEOCHROMOCYTOMA
content:
Catecholamine-secreting tumor of the adrenal medulla.

• Triad: Episodic Headache, Sweating, and Palpitations.
• Screening: 24-hr Urinary Metanephrines or Plasma fractionated metanephrines.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Pre-operative prep is CRITICAL: Give Alpha-blocker (Phenoxybenzamine) FIRST, then Beta-blockers. If you give Beta-blockers first, the 'unopposed alpha' will cause a fatal hypertensive crisis.

quiz.q: In what order should medications be given before pheochromocytoma surgery?

title: 🧠 SEROTONIN SYNDROME VS NMS
content:
FeatureSerotonin SyndromeNMS
CauseSSRIs / MAOIsAntipsychotics
ReflexesHyperreflexia / ClonusHyporeflexia / Rigidity
OnsetSudden (< 24h)Gradual (Days)

⚠️ Exam Trap: Lead-pipe rigidity is the hallmark of NMS. Clonus (shaking limbs) is the hallmark of Serotonin Syndrome. Both are medical emergencies requiring drug discontinuation and cooling.

quiz.q: Which syndrome presents with hyperreflexia and neuromuscular clonus?

title: 👁️ CHEMICAL EYE BURNS
content:
Ocular Emergency! Acid or Alkali splash.

• Absolute First Step: Immediate Irrigation with saline or water for at least 30 mins (until pH is 7.0 - 7.2).
• Note: Alkali (bleach, drain cleaner) is worse because it causes liquefactive necrosis and deep penetration.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT wait to check vision (Acuity) or call a specialist. Irrigate first, then assess the damage. This is one of the few times acuity is NOT the first step in eye cases.

quiz.q: What is the priority management for a chemical burn to the eye?

title: 🧪 CARBON MONOXIDE (CO) POISONING
content:
The 'Silent Killer'.

• Signs: Headache, dizziness, nausea, 'Cherry-red' skin (late/rare).
• Labs: Normal SpO2 (pulse ox is fake!), high Carboxyhemoglobin level.
• Tx: 100% Hyperbaric Oxygen (if severe/pregnant).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Pulse oximetry cannot distinguish between HgbO2 and HgbCO. A patient can have a Normal SpO2 and still be dying from CO poisoning. Check an ABG for carboxyhemoglobin!

quiz.q: What is the specific test to diagnose carbon monoxide poisoning?
quiz.a: Co-oximetry (Carboxyhemoglobin level) via ABG or VBG.

title: 🧴 SCC VS BCC VS MELANOMA
content:
FeatureBasal Cell (BCC)Squamous Cell (SCC)Melanoma
LookPearly / TelangiectasiaScaly / CrustedPigmented / ABCDE
RiskSun exposureSun / ImmunosuppressionSun / Family history

⚠️ Exam Trap: SCC can arise from Actinic Keratosis and is more aggressive than BCC. In a transplant patient, SCC is the most common and dangerous skin cancer.

quiz.q: Which skin cancer is most commonly associated with immunosuppression?

title: 🩸 SMEAR CHALLENGE: SCHISTOCYTES
content:
• Significance: Suggests MAHA (Microangiopathic Hemolytic Anemia).
• Differential: TTP, HUS, DIC, or Mechanical valves.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If you see Schistocytes + Fever + Confusion + Low Platelets, the next best step is Emergent Plasmapheresis (TTP). Do not wait for ADAMTS13 levels!

quiz.q: What do schistocytes on a peripheral smear indicate?

title: ⚖️ ELDER ABUSE REPORTING
content:
A sensitive Canadian legal requirement.

• Rule: Unlike child abuse (which is mandatory everywhere), reporting elder abuse to authorities varies by province, but investigating and protecting the patient is always mandatory.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If an elderly patient with Capacity refuses to let you report abuse, you must respect their Autonomy (unless it's in a long-term care home, where reporting is usually mandatory). Always offer resources/social work.

quiz.q: Is reporting elder abuse always legally mandatory if the patient has full capacity?
quiz.a: No (depends on province/setting), but ensuring safety is mandatory.

title: 🍔 AAA SCREENING (CANADA)
content:
Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm screening guidelines.

• Who: Men aged 65 - 80 (One-time screen).
• How: Abdominal Ultrasound.
• Management:
- < 5.0 cm: Follow with serial U/S.
- > 5.5 cm: Refer for Repair (EVAR or Open).

⚠️ Exam Trap: For Women, there is no routine screening recommendation unless they have high-risk factors (Smoking/Family Hx).

quiz.q: What is the threshold size for recommending surgical repair of an AAA in a man?

title: 🧪 CKD MANAGEMENT
content:
Chronic Kidney Disease: Slowing progression.

• Target BP: < 130/80 mmHg.
• First-line Med: ACEi or ARB (especially if albuminuria present).
• Complications: Anemia (Low EPO), Bone disease (High Phos, Low Vit D).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In CKD, the kidneys stop activating Vitamin D and stop excreting Phosphorus. This leads to Secondary Hyperparathyroidism. Treatment is Phosphate binders and Calcitriol.

quiz.q: Which class of antihypertensives is renal-protective in patients with CKD and proteinuria?

title: 🦠 TUBERCULOSIS (PPD / IGRA)
content:
Screening for latent TB.

• Positive Test: Induration ≥ 10mm (average risk) or ≥ 5mm (high risk/HIV).
• Next Best Step: Chest X-ray to rule out active disease.
• If CXR negative: Treat for Latent TB (Isoniazid for 9 months).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has had the BCG vaccine, the PPD might be false positive. In this case, use IGRA (QuantiFERON) as it is not affected by BCG.

quiz.q: What is the next step for a patient with a positive PPD test?
quiz.a: Chest X-ray (to rule out active TB).

title: ⚖️ POWER OF ATTORNEY (POA)
content:
TypeResponsibility
POA for Personal CareHealth decisions, housing, food.
POA for PropertyFinances, bills, bank accounts.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A physician only consults the POA for Personal Care for medical decisions. The fact that someone has POA for Property does NOT give them the right to make health decisions!

quiz.q: Which Power of Attorney is responsible for making medical decisions for an incapable patient?
quiz.a: Power of Attorney for Personal Care.

title: 🏆 MISSION COMPLETE: MCCQE1 MINDSET
content:
Congratulations, Doctor! You now have a 500-card elite vault.

• Final Strategy:
1. Safety First: Always stabilize (ABCs) before diagnosing.
2. Autonomy: Respect the capable patient's choice, even if you disagree.
3. Canadian Rules: Think within the Canadian public health and legal framework.

🌟 THE VAULT IS CLOSED 🌟
You are ready. Trust your knowledge, stay calm, and dominate the exam. You've got this, Canada's future physician!

quiz.q: What is the physician's first priority in any trauma or emergency case?

title: ⚖️ ACUTE VS CHRONIC LIVER FAILURE
content:
FeatureAcute (Fulminant)Chronic (Cirrhosis)
HistorySudden (Drugs/Viral)Long-term (Alcohol/NASH)
BrainCerebral EdemaHepatic Encephalopathy
SpleenNormal sizeSplenomegaly (Portal HTN)

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Acute liver failure, the leading cause of death is Cerebral Edema (increased ICP). In Chronic, it is Variceal bleeding or Sepsis.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of death in acute fulminant hepatic failure?

title: 👶 PEDIATRIC RASHES: THE 'ORDER' MNEMONIC
content:
1. Measles: 3 C's (Cough, Coryza, Conjunctivitis) + Koplik spots.
2. Scarlet: Sandpaper rash + Strawberry tongue.
3. Rubella: Lymphadenopathy (Post-auricular).
4. Duke: (Obsolete).
5. Erythema Infectiosum: Slapped cheek.
6. Roseola: High fever, THEN rash.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Only Measles and Rubella start on the face and spread downwards. Differentiate them by the 3 C's (Measles is much sicker!).

quiz.q: Which pediatric rash is preceded by a high fever that disappears before the rash starts?

title: 🎗️ BONE METASTASES: THE LIKELY SOURCE
content:
Lesion TypeLikely Primary Cancer
Osteoblastic (White)Prostate, Small Cell Lung.
Osteolytic (Dark/Punched)MM, RCC, Thyroid, Non-small Cell Lung.
MixedBreast (Most common source in women).


⚠️ Exam Trap: If an elderly man has Back Pain and a high ALP with blastic (white) lesions on X-ray, the next best step is a PSA and Digital Rectal Exam (Prostate Cancer).

quiz.q: Which cancer characteristically causes osteoblastic (sclerotic) bone metastases?

title: 🧪 HYPOKALEMIA: ECG CHANGES
content:
The Pattern:
1. Flattened T-waves.
2. U-waves (Extra wave after T).
3. ST Depression.
4. Prolonged QT.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If Hypokalemia is refractory to treatment (K+ won't go up), the next best step is to check Magnesium levels. Low Magnesium prevents K+ from being reabsorbed in the kidney.

quiz.q: What is the classic ECG wave seen in severe hypokalemia?

title: ⚖️ THE IMPAIRED COLLEAGUE
content:
A senior doctor smells of alcohol or is behaving erratically.

• Principle: Patient Safety is the absolute priority.
• Action: You must report them to the appropriate authority (Chief of Staff or Provincial Regulatory Body/CPSO).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Talking to the colleague 'privately' or 'covering' for them is the WRONG answer. In Canada, reporting an impaired colleague is a professional and legal obligation.

quiz.q: What is the first priority when you suspect a colleague is practicing under the influence of substances?

title: 🦠 CELLULITIS VS ERYSIPELAS
content:
FeatureCellulitisErysipelas
LayerDeep Dermis / SubcutaneousSuperficial Dermis
BordersIndistinct / DiffuseSharp / Well-demarcated
PathogenS. aureus / Group A StrepGroup A Strep (almost always)


⚠️ Exam Trap: Erysipelas is more likely to involve the Ear (Milian's ear sign) because the ear has no subcutaneous tissue. Cellulitis cannot involve the earlobe!

quiz.q: Which skin infection is characterized by sharp, well-demarcated borders and rapid onset?

title: 🧠 DELIRIUM: THE INITIAL WORKUP
content:
Acute change in consciousness and attention.

• Next Best Step: Identify and treat the underlying cause.
• Workup: Vital signs, Glucose, UA/Culture (UTI is #1 in elderly), Electrolytes, Medication review.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Avoid Benzodiazepines (they worsen delirium) unless it is due to Alcohol Withdrawal. If the patient is a danger to themselves, use low-dose Antipsychotics (Haloperidol).

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of delirium in an elderly patient?
quiz.a: Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) or medication side effects.

title: 🧱 CHOLE-DIFFERENTIALS
content:
ConditionDefinitionNext Best Step
CholelithiasisStone in GB (No infection)Reassurance / Elective surgery
CholecystitisInfection of GBUltrasound → Surgery
CholedocholithiasisStone in CBD (Jaundice)ERCP
CholangitisInfection of CBD (Pentad)Emergency ERCP

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has Jaundice (High Bilirubin), the stone is not in the gallbladder; it must be in the Common Bile Duct (CBD).

quiz.q: What is the definitive treatment for Ascending Cholangitis?

title: 🌬️ PFT INTERPRETATION
content:
PatternFEV1/FVC RatioTLC (Total Lung Capacity)
Obstructive (Asthma/COPD)Low (< 0.7)High / Normal
Restrictive (Fibrosis)Normal / HighLow


⚠️ Exam Trap: To differentiate Asthma from COPD, check Reversibility: an increase in FEV1 by > 12% and > 200mL after bronchodilator = Asthma.

quiz.q: In which type of lung disease is the FEV1/FVC ratio characteristically decreased?

title: ⚖️ RESOURCE ALLOCATION
content:
How to decide who gets a limited resource (e.g., Ventilator, Organ).

• Principle: Distributive Justice.
• Criteria: Medical need, likelihood of benefit, and urgency.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You must NOT use social criteria (e.g., 'this patient is a doctor', 'this patient has children', 'this patient is wealthy') to prioritize care. Only Clinical Factors matter in the Canadian system.

quiz.q: Is it ethical to prioritize a patient for an organ transplant because they are a prominent member of society?
quiz.a: No, it is unethical and violates the principle of justice.

title: 🚑 ANAPHYLAXIS: STEP 1
content:
Sudden onset of respiratory distress/hypotension after trigger.

• Absolute First Step: IM Epinephrine (1:1000) in the mid-outer thigh.
• Next Steps: Oxygen, IV Fluids, Antihistamines/Steroids (secondary).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Never waste time giving IV Epinephrine or Antihistamines first. The priority is IM Epinephrine. Airway management (Intubation) is next if there is impending obstruction.

quiz.q: What is the dose and route of Epinephrine in acute anaphylaxis?

title: 🫀 HEART FAILURE: THE 'BIG 4' DRUGS
content:
Current Canadian (CCS) guidelines for HFrEF (EF < 40%).

The 4 Pillars:
1. ARNI (Sacubitril/Valsartan) or ACEi/ARB.
2. Beta-blocker (Bisoprolol, Carvedilol, Metoprolol succinate).
3. MRA (Spironolactone).
4. SGLT2i (Dapagliflozin/Empagliflozin).

⚠️ Exam Trap: All 4 pillars have proven Mortality Benefit. If a patient is stable on an ACEi, the next best step is to switch to ARNI to further reduce mortality.

quiz.q: Which class of diabetes medications is now a pillar of treatment for Heart Failure even in non-diabetics?

title: 🦴 OPEN FRACTURES
content:
A fracture with an overlying skin wound.

• Next Best Step: Antibiotics + Tetanus Prophylaxis + Irrigating/Covering the wound.
• Definitive: Urgent I&D (Incision and Drainage) in the OR.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The sooner the antibiotics are given (ideally < 1 hour), the lower the risk of Osteomyelitis. Use Cefazolin (add Gentamicin for larger wounds).

quiz.q: What is the most important medical intervention to prevent infection in an open fracture?

title: 🦠 PELVIC INFLAMMATORY DISEASE (PID)
content:
ScenarioManagement
Mild/ModerateOutpatient: Ceftriaxone (IM) + Doxycycline (14d)
Severe / AbscessInpatient: IV Cefotetan + Doxycycline

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has a Tubo-ovarian Abscess (TOA), is pregnant, or is not responding to oral meds, Admission for IV antibiotics is the next best step.

quiz.q: What is the first-line outpatient antibiotic regimen for PID?
quiz.a: Single dose IM Ceftriaxone plus 14 days of oral Doxycycline.

title: 🦋 THYROID CANCER: MEDULLARY
content:
Cancer of the C-cells (produces Calcitonin).

• Association: MEN 2A / 2B (RET proto-oncogene).
• Next Best Step (once diagnosed): Screen for Pheochromocytoma (Metanephrines).

⚠️ Exam Trap: You MUST rule out and treat a Pheochromocytoma before performing thyroid surgery. An undiagnosed 'Pheo' can cause a fatal hypertensive crisis during anesthesia.

quiz.q: Which syndrome is associated with Medullary Thyroid Cancer?

title: 👶 INTUSSUSCEPTION: TX
content:
Telescoping of the bowel; sudden abdominal pain + 'Currant jelly' stool.

• Next Best Step: Air or Contrast Enema (both diagnostic and therapeutic).
• Note: If signs of peritonitis/perforation, go straight to surgery.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Ultrasound is the best initial diagnostic test (Target sign). The Enema is the next best step to treat it.

quiz.q: What is the non-surgical treatment of choice for intussusception?

title: 🩸 TTP (PENTAD)
content:
Deficiency of ADAMTS13.

FAT RN Mnemonic:
1. Fever.
2. Anemia (MAHA/Schistocytes).
3. Thrombocytopenia.
4. Renal failure.
5. Neurological symptoms.

⚠️ Exam Trap: This is a Medical Emergency. Next best step: Plasmapheresis (Plasma exchange). Never wait for labs! Never give Platelets (it 'fuels the fire' of thrombosis).

quiz.q: What is the immediate treatment for suspected Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP)?

title: 🗣️ THE GOSSIPING STAFF
content:
You overhear a nurse or doctor discussing a patient's private details in the elevator/cafeteria.

• Principle: Confidentiality.
• Next Best Step: Intervene immediately but politely. Remind them that the elevator is a public space and they are violating patient privacy.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do not wait to report them later. The priority is to Stop the breach now.

quiz.q: What should you do if you witness a colleague discussing a patient in a public area?
quiz.a: Immediately and politely intervene to stop the discussion.

title: 🦠 SEPSIS: THE 1-HOUR BUNDLE
content:
Critical management in the first hour.

1. Measure Lactate.
2. Obtain Blood Cultures (Before antibiotics).
3. Administer Broad-spectrum Antibiotics.
4. Rapid administration of 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the question asks for the 'Next Step' and both cultures and antibiotics are options: Cultures first, then immediately Antibiotics. Do NOT delay antibiotics if cultures are taking too long.

quiz.q: Should blood cultures be obtained before or after starting antibiotics in sepsis?
quiz.a: Before (but do not delay antibiotics significantly).

title: 💎 THE DIAMOND SUMMARY: 5 TOP TRAPS
content:
The most important 'Final' traps to remember:

1. Shoulder Dystocia: McRoberts maneuver + Suprapubic pressure. (No fundal pressure!).
2. DKA: Check Potassium before starting Insulin.
3. Stroke: CT Head (non-contrast) before Aspirin/tPA.
4. Cervical Spine: Clear it clinically (NEXUS/Canadian rules) before removing the collar.
5. Domestic Violence: Your role is to Assess Safety and provide resources; you cannot report it for a capable adult without their consent.

🌟 CONGRATULATIONS DOCTOR! 🌟
You have reached the final card (520). Your Imperial Vault is now complete and unique. You have all the tools to succeed. Go and make Canada proud!

quiz.q: Can you report domestic violence of a capable adult to the police without their consent in Canada?
quiz.a: No, you must respect their autonomy (unlike child or elder abuse in some provinces).

title: 🏭 LEAD POISONING (OCCUPATIONAL)
content:
A 45-year-old worker in a battery recycling plant presents with irritability and vague abdominal pain. He rarely uses protective gear.

• Next Best Step: Determine Blood Lead Levels (Plombémie).
• Note: Blood lead is the most effective way to assess recent absorption. Anemia, wrist drop, and low sperm counts are late findings.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A 24-hour urinary lead test measures exposure but does not replace blood lead levels as the primary diagnostic tool in the early stage.[span_0](end_span)[span_1](end_span)

quiz.q: What is the most effective initial test to evaluate recent lead exposure?

title: 🦋 ADRENAL INCIDENTALOMA (HTN)
content:
A 52-year-old woman is found to have a 3.2 cm adrenal mass on CT (incidental). She has controlled hypertension on a thiazide-like diuretic.

• Initial Step: Calculate the Plasma Aldosterone/Renin Ratio (ARR).
• Note: Primary hyperaldosteronism screening is recommended for ALL patients with hypertension and an adrenal incidentaloma.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Biopsy is NOT indicated for masses < 4 cm unless there's a history of extra-adrenal malignancy. [span_2](start_span)[span_3](start_span)Adrenal vein sampling is a later step for lateralization.[span_2](end_span)[span_3](end_span)

quiz.q: What is the recommended screening test for primary aldosteronism in a patient with an adrenal mass?

title: 🌙 NOCTURNAL ENURESIS (IMMEDIATE NEED)
content:
An 11-year-old girl wants to attend a 2-week summer camp starting in 7 days and is anxious about bedwetting.

• Best Management: Prescribe Desmopressin.
• Logic: While enuresis alarms are the best long-term fix (taking months), Desmopressin provides immediate dryness for specific events like camps.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Imipramine (TCA) is only used when other treatments fail due to its high side-effect profile (cardiotoxicity).[span_4](end_span)[span_5](end_span)

quiz.q: Which medication is most effective for an child needing immediate nighttime dryness for a short-term event?

title: ⚖️ MAID & SEVERE DEMENTIA
content:
A 78-year-old with severe dementia is brought by her son (SDM). [span_6](start_span)[span_7](start_span)He states she always wanted Medical Assistance in Dying (MAID) once she lost interaction capability.

• Fact: Official Assessment of decision-making capacity and health status is mandatory at the current time.
• Note: In Canada, a patient must be mentally capable to give/withdraw consent at the time of the procedure unless a very specific 'final arrangement' was made while capable.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A prior request or a son's consent is insufficient if the patient cannot give current capable consent or hasn't met all legal criteria.[span_6](end_span)[span_7](end_span)

quiz.q: Can a substitute decision-maker (son) consent to MAID on behalf of an incapable parent?
quiz.a: No, the patient must meet specific capacity and legal requirements themselves.

title: 🗣️ DISCLOSING MEDICAL ERRORS
content:
You prescribe a dose of insulin (45 units) instead of the intended 25 units. [span_8](start_span)[span_9](start_span)The patient receives it but experiences no harm.

• Action: Immediately inform the patient and apologize.
• Ethics: Disclosure is required for any error that reaches the patient, regardless of whether harm occurred.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the error was caught before reaching the patient (Near Miss), disclosure to the patient is usually not mandatory but internal reporting is.[span_8](end_span)[span_9](end_span)

quiz.q: Should a physician disclose a medication error if the patient suffered no adverse effects?
quiz.a: Yes, disclosure is mandatory for errors that reach the patient.

title: 🤫 THE FAMOUS PATIENT
content:
A colleague asks you about a 'prominent local personality' under your care for cancer.

• Action: Neither deny nor acknowledge that the person is your patient.
• Principle: Confirming someone is a patient (even without clinical details) is a breach of confidentiality.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Simply saying 'I can't talk about the case' is wrong because it confirms they are your patient. [span_10](start_span)[span_11](start_span)The correct phrase is to neither deny nor confirm.[span_10](end_span)[span_11](end_span)

quiz.q: What should a physician say when a colleague asks if a specific celebrity is their patient?
quiz.a: Neither deny nor acknowledge that the individual is under their care.

title: 🧠 POST-OP DELIRIUM (PROGNOSIS)
content:
An 85-year-old woman is agitated and hallucinating 3 days after hip surgery. [span_12](start_span)[span_13](start_span)Her family is distressed as she was previously independent.

• Communication: Inform family that symptoms usually improve in days/weeks with proper management of the underlying cause.
• Fact: 1-year mortality after a delirium episode can reach 50%.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT delegate this communication to nursing staff; the physician must explain the prognosis and management of delirium.[span_12](end_span)[span_13](end_span)

quiz.q: What is the 1-year mortality rate for patients who experience an episode of delirium?

title: 🦴 SHORT STATURE (CONSTITUTIONAL)
content:
A 14-year-old boy is in the 3rd percentile for height. He is Tanner Stage 1. His parents are average height. [span_14](start_span)[span_15](start_span)His mother had menarche at 15.

• Best Investigation: Bone Age Radiography.
• Diagnosis: Constitutional Delay of Growth and Puberty (CDGP).
• Finding: Bone age will be delayed (less than chronological age).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Growth hormone levels or karyotyping are NOT initial tests for a healthy child with a family history of 'late blooming'.[span_14](end_span)[span_15](end_span)

quiz.q: Which investigation supports a diagnosis of constitutional delay of growth and puberty?

title: 🏳️‍⚧️ TRANSGENDER CARE (INITIAL)
content:
A 15-year-old patient known as female comes for a visit and identifies as a transgender boy.

• Next Best Step: Ask the patient how they prefer to be addressed.
• Principle: Patient-centered care and building trust are the priorities before medical or psychiatric referrals.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT immediately refer to an endocrinologist or psychiatrist as the 'next step' without first establishing a relationship and addressing their preferred identity.[span_16](end_span)[span_17](end_span)

quiz.q: What is the first step in building a professional relationship with a transgender patient?
quiz.a: Ask how they prefer to be addressed.

title: 🦋 GRAVES & OPHTHALMOPATHY
content:
A 75-year-old woman has weight loss, tachycardia (125/min), and Exophthalmos. [span_18](start_span)[span_19](start_span)TSH is 0.08.

• Initial Step: Symptom control with Propylthiouracil (PTU) and Propranolol.
• Note: Radioactive iodine ablation is usually avoided in patients with moderate to severe ophthalmopathy.

⚠️ Exam Trap: While TSH-receptor antibodies confirm Graves, the most appropriate step in an acute elderly patient is symptom management.[span_18](end_span)[span_19](end_span)

quiz.q: What initial medical combination is used for symptom control in a patient with Graves' hyperthyroidism?

title: 🧠 CONVERSION DISORDER
content:
A 35-year-old woman has sudden bilateral leg paralysis. Neuro exam shows normal tone and sensation, but she cannot stand. [span_20](start_span)[span_21](start_span)She is undergoing a difficult divorce.

• Likely Diagnosis: Conversion Disorder.
• Logic: Symptoms of motor/sensory loss that are incompatible with any known neurological or medical condition.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Somatic Symptom Disorder involves distress about physical symptoms, but Conversion Disorder specifically involves 'neurological' deficits with no organic cause.[span_20](end_span)[span_21](end_span)

quiz.q: Which diagnosis is characterized by motor or sensory symptoms incompatible with neurological disease?

title: 🫁 LUNG CANCER SCREENING (CANADA)
content:
A 58-year-old man with a 35 pack-year history quit smoking 10 years ago. He is worried about cancer.

• Management: Annual Low-dose CT of the chest for 3 consecutive years.
• Eligibility: Age 55-74, Current smoker or quit within 15 years, ≥ 30 pack-year history.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A normal X-ray is not enough to rule out screening; [span_22](start_span)[span_23](start_span)LDCT is the only evidence-based screening tool for high-risk smokers.[span_22](end_span)[span_23](end_span)

quiz.q: What is the evidence-based screening investigation for lung cancer in high-risk patients?
quiz.a: Annual low-dose computed tomography (LDCT) for 3 years.

title: 🤰 PRENATAL CHOICE: DNA SCREENING
content:
A 39-year-old gravida 1 para 0 wants to pay for private Cell-free DNA (cfDNA) screening instead of the provincial serum screen.

• Next Best Step: Assist her in obtaining the cfDNA screening.
• Logic: All pregnant women in Canada should be offered aneuploidy screening; patients have the right to choose higher-accuracy tests, even if privately funded.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT discourage cfDNA. [span_24](start_span)[span_25](start_span)It is > 99% accurate for Trisomy 21 and safer than invasive tests.[span_24](end_span)[span_25](end_span)

quiz.q: Should a physician assist a patient in obtaining a privately funded cfDNA screen if they request it?
quiz.a: Yes, if they understand the benefits and potential lack of provincial funding.

title: 🧠 NMS CONFIRMATION
content:
A 54-year-old with schizophrenia on Haloperidol/Fluphenazine has high fever ($39^{\circ}C$), BP 210/130, and Muscle Rigidity.

• Best Confirmation: Serum Creatine Kinase (CK) level.
• Differential: Serotonin Syndrome (Hyperreflexia/Clonus) vs NMS (Rigidity/Lead-pipe).

⚠️ Exam Trap: CT head or drug screens won't confirm NMS; the hallmark is extremely high CK due to rigid muscle breakdown.[span_26](end_span)[span_27](end_span)

quiz.q: Which lab test is most helpful in confirming neuroleptic malignant syndrome?

title: 🧠 SUICIDE: STRONGEST RISK FACTOR
content:
A 35-year-old man with depression, heavy alcohol use, and a history of two prior hospitalizations for suicide attempts.

• Highest Risk Factor: Previous suicide attempts.
• Note: Substance use and gender (male) increase risk, but history of attempts is the #1 predictor of future completion.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Marital status (having a spouse) is actually a protective factor, not a risk factor.[span_28](end_span)[span_29](end_span)

quiz.q: What is the single most significant risk factor for dying by suicide?

title: 🍕 BINGE EATING DISORDER (BED)
content:
A 34-year-old morbidly obese woman impulsively eats large amounts of food twice a week. She does not purge.

• Best Treatment: Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT).
• Note: CBT is more effective for BED symptoms than pharmacotherapy (Lisdexamfetamine/Topiramate).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT just refer to a dietitian; [span_30](start_span)BED is a psychiatric disorder requiring structured therapy (CBT or IPT).[span_30](end_span)

quiz.q: What is the first-line evidence-based treatment for binge-eating disorder?

title: 🧴 BASAL CELL CARCINOMA (BCC) TX
content:
A 72-year-old has a 10 mm pearly, ulcerated lesion on his forearm (high sun exposure).

• Appropriate Management: Local Excision (< 1 cm margin).
• Fact: BCC metastasis is extremely rare. [span_31](start_span)Wide margins (2 cm) are for melanoma.

⚠️ Exam Trap: CT staging or lymph node dissection is never required for standard BCC.[span_31](end_span)

quiz.q: What is the standard surgical margin for a local excision of a basal cell carcinoma?

title: 🎻 LITHIUM TREMOR
content:
A professional violinist stable on Lithium develops a hand tremor that interferes with performance.

• Best Management: Propranolol.
• Note: Benztropine/Amantadine treat extrapyramidal signs (from antipsychotics), not lithium-induced fine tremors.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Always check if the lithium level is within therapeutic range first. [span_32](start_span)If the tremor is new and severe, check for Toxicity.[span_32](end_span)

quiz.q: Which medication is first-line for treating a lithium-induced hand tremor?

title: 🍭 INSULINOMA VS FACTITIOUS
content:
A 45-year-old with fasting hypoglycemia. Labs show High Insulin and High C-peptide.

• Likely Diagnosis: Insulinoma.
• Logic: High C-peptide indicates Endogenous insulin production. If C-peptide were low, it would suggest exogenous insulin (Factitious).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Metformin does not cause high insulin levels; it increases sensitivity. [span_33](start_span)[span_34](start_span)Only insulin or secretagogues (sulfonylureas) cause high insulin.[span_33](end_span)[span_34](end_span)

quiz.q: What laboratory marker distinguishes endogenous hyperinsulinemia from exogenous insulin use?

title: 🤰 RhD STATUS (JEHOVAH'S WITNESS)
content:
A 29-year-old RhD-negative Jehovah's Witness wants to avoid Rh-immune globulin unless absolutely necessary.

• Next Best Step: Offer Cell-free DNA screening to determine fetal RhD status.
• Benefit: If the fetus is RhD-negative, she can safely avoid the injection. cfDNA is > 99% accurate.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT recommend invasive tests (Amnio/CVS) just for Rh status due to the risk of fetal-maternal hemorrhage worsening alloimmunization.[span_35](end_span)

quiz.q: What non-invasive test can determine fetal RhD genotype to avoid unnecessary prophylaxis?

title: 🧠 CULTURAL CONCEPT OF DISTRESS
content:
A 44-year-old Japanese immigrant avoids work because she believes she has an 'offensive body odor', despite no objective smell. She is highly anxious.

• Next Best Step: Assess her distress in relation to the norms of her cultural reference group.
• Logic: In certain cultures (e.g., Japan), social anxiety can manifest as Taijin Kyofusho (fear of offending others with one's body).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do not immediately diagnose 'Delusional Disorder' or 'OCD' without first performing a Cultural Formulation to see if the symptoms are a culturally-specific expression of anxiety.

quiz.q: How should a physician assess an immigrant patient presenting with distress that seems culturally specific?
quiz.a: Assess the distress in relation to the norms of the patient’s cultural reference group.

title: 👶 PHYSIOLOGICAL ANEMIA OF INFANCY
content:
A 6-week-old healthy infant has a hemoglobin of $140~g/L$. At birth, it was $220~g/L$. She is growing well and exclusively breastfed.

• Likely Diagnosis: Normal physiological changes.
• Logic: After birth, oxygenation improves, EPO production drops, and fetal RBCs have a shorter lifespan, leading to a nadir (lowest point) around 6-12 weeks.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT start iron supplementation or investigate for occult blood if the child is thriving and the Hb is within the expected range for their age ($100-140~g/L$ at 2 months).

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of a drop in hemoglobin in a healthy 6-week-old infant?

title: 🤰 SUPPORT PERSONS IN LABOR
content:
A woman in active labor wants her mother, sister, and a doula present in the room. The nurse is concerned about the physical space.

• MCC Principle: Whomever the patient would like to be present should be allowed, respecting her wishes and autonomy.
• Action: The physician should advocate for institutional protocols that support the family's presence.

⚠️ Exam Trap: There is NO rule in Canada that 'only women' or 'only two people' can be present. The focus is on patient-centered care.

quiz.q: Who is allowed to provide support for a woman during labor and delivery?
quiz.a: Whomever the patient would like to be present.

title: 🕊️ CHRONIC PAIN IN CANCER
content:
A patient with metastatic prostate cancer has worsening bone pain despite being on a short-acting opioid. His mood is low and he is anxious.

• Next Best Step: Combine Long-acting and Short-acting (breakthrough) opioids.
• Logic: For continuous cancer pain, a long-acting background dose is essential for stability.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do not just increase the short-acting dose. If the pain is constant, the combination is the gold standard. Address the 'Total Pain' (physical, psychological, and social).

quiz.q: What is the preferred opioid regimen for managing constant cancer-related pain?
quiz.a: A combination of long-acting (scheduled) and short-acting (PRN) opioids.

title: ⚖️ REFUSING TREATMENT (CAPACITY)
content:
A 25-year-old man with a severe, treatable infection refuses antibiotics because he 'doesn't like drugs'. He is oriented and understands the risk of death.

• Action: Respect his refusal.
• Rule: A mentally capable adult has the right to refuse any medical treatment, even if that refusal leads to death.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT assume lack of capacity just because a decision seems 'unwise' or 'irrational'. Capacity is determined by the ability to understand and appreciate the consequences.

quiz.q: Can a mentally capable patient refuse life-saving treatment in Canada?
quiz.a: Yes, autonomy allows for the refusal of any treatment if the patient is capable.

title: 🧱 ACUTE CHOLECYSTITIS: NEXT STEP
content:
A 40-year-old woman presents with RUQ pain, fever, and a positive Murphy's sign. Labs show leukocytosis and mild elevation of ALP.

• Best Initial Test: Ultrasound of the Abdomen.
• Definitive Step: Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy (usually during the same admission).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the ultrasound is inconclusive but suspicion is high, the next best step is a HIDA scan (Cholescintigraphy), which is the most sensitive test for cholecystitis.

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive imaging test for diagnosing acute cholecystitis?

title: 👶 CROUP (LARYNGOTRACHEOBRONCHITIS)
content:
A 2-year-old presents with a 'barky' cough and inspiratory stridor at rest.

• Management: Dexamethasone (Oral or IM) + Nebulized Epinephrine (if stridor at rest).
• Note: X-rays are usually NOT needed for diagnosis but may show the 'Steeple Sign'.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the child is stable, do not agitate them (e.g., trying to visualize the throat) as it can worsen the airway obstruction.

quiz.q: What is the first-line medical treatment for a child with moderate-to-severe Croup?

title: 🩸 B12 DEFICIENCY (LABS)
content:
A 70-year-old vegan presents with paresthesia and macrocytic anemia. B12 level is 'low-normal'.

• Next Best Step: Check Methylmalonic Acid (MMA) levels.
• Note: MMA is a more sensitive marker for tissue-level B12 deficiency than a serum B12 level.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Folate deficiency can also cause macrocytic anemia but will NOT cause Neurological symptoms (paresthesia/vibratory sense loss). In B12 deficiency, both MMA and Homocysteine are high.

quiz.q: Which lab test is most sensitive for confirming Vitamin B12 deficiency when the serum level is borderline?

title: 📊 ODDS RATIO (OR) CALCULATION
content:
Used in Case-Control studies to estimate the strength of association.

$$OR = rac{A imes D}{B imes C}$$
• A: Exposed cases. D: Unexposed controls.
• B: Exposed controls. C: Unexposed cases.

⚠️ Exam Trap: An OR = 1 means no association. An OR > 1 means the exposure is a risk factor. An OR < 1 means the exposure is protective.

quiz.q: In which type of study is the Odds Ratio (OR) primarily used?

title: 🦠 INFECTIOUS MONONUCLEOSIS (EBV)
content:
A 19-year-old student has fever, severe sore throat, and posterior cervical lymphadenopathy.

• Diagnosis: Monospot (Heterophile antibody) test.
• Advice: Avoid contact sports for 3-4 weeks to prevent Splenic Rupture.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If you mistakenly give Amoxicillin for suspected Strep throat, the patient may develop a maculopapular Rash. This is NOT a true allergy but a common reaction in EBV.

quiz.q: What is the most important piece of lifestyle advice for a patient with infectious mononucleosis?
quiz.a: Avoid contact sports (due to risk of splenic rupture).

title: 🧠 BULIMIA NERVOSA (LABS)
content:
A 22-year-old woman has dental enamel erosion and parotid gland swelling.

• Classic Lab Finding: Hypokalemic Hypochloremic Metabolic Alkalosis.
• Reason: Loss of gastric acid (HCl) through repetitive self-induced vomiting.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Bulimia patients are usually normal weight or slightly overweight, unlike Anorexia patients who have a BMI < 18.5. Fluoxetine is the only FDA-approved SSRI for Bulimia.

quiz.q: What is the typical electrolyte and acid-base abnormality seen in self-induced vomiting?

title: 🧠 PARKINSON’S DISEASE (TRIAD)
content:
The Cardinal Features:
1. Resting Tremor ('Pill-rolling').
2. Bradykinesia (Slow movement - Most disabling).
3. Rigidity ('Lead-pipe' or 'Cogwheel').
4. Postural instability (later).

⚠️ Exam Trap: The tremor is Asymmetrical at onset. Diagnosis is Clinical; you do not need an MRI to diagnose Parkinson's unless you suspect something else.

quiz.q: Which cardinal feature of Parkinson's is considered the most disabling for the patient?

title: 🤰 LITHIUM IN PREGNANCY
content:
A woman with bipolar disorder is planning pregnancy.

• Risk: Ebstein’s Anomaly (Tricuspid valve defect) in the first trimester.
• Management: If the disease is stable, consider tapering off. If severe, stay on lithium but perform detailed Fetal Echocardiogram at 18-20 weeks.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Lithium is not an absolute contraindication, but the dose needs to be monitored closely as GFR increases during pregnancy, leading to lower lithium levels.

quiz.q: What is the specific cardiac malformation associated with first-trimester Lithium exposure?

title: 🧴 TINEA CORPORIS (RINGWORM)
content:
Annular (ring-shaped) erythematous plaque with central clearing and a scaly border.

• Diagnosis: Clinical or KOH prep showing hyphae.
• Tx: Topical Antifungals (Clotrimazole/Terbinafine).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT use topical steroids (like Hydrocortisone) alone; it will lead to 'Tinea Incognito' (the rash gets worse and spreads). Steroids mask the inflammation but feed the fungus.

quiz.q: What is the definitive initial treatment for a localized ringworm rash?

title: 🔪 INGUINAL HERNIA (TYPES)
content:
TypeAnatomyPatient
IndirectThrough internal ring (Lateral to inferior epigastric)Young males (Congenital)
DirectThrough Hesselbach triangle (Medial to inferior epigastric)Older males (Weakness)

⚠️ Exam Trap: Indirect hernia is the most common in both sexes. If the hernia cannot be reduced and is painful, it is Incarcerated (Needs surgical evaluation). If there is fever/leukocytosis, it is Strangulated (Emergency!).

quiz.q: Which type of hernia passes through the inguinal canal and enters the scrotum?

title: 🧱 ACHALASIA
content:
Failure of the Lower Esophageal Sphincter (LES) to relax.

• Symptoms: Dysphagia to both solids and liquids, regurgitation of undigested food.
• Dx: 1st: Barium swallow (Bird's beak). Gold Standard: Manometry.

⚠️ Exam Trap: All patients with new-onset achalasia symptoms must have Endoscopy to rule out 'Pseudo-achalasia' caused by gastric or esophageal cancer.

quiz.q: What is the gold standard diagnostic test for Achalasia?

title: 🦴 NURSEMAID’S ELBOW
content:
Radial head subluxation caused by sudden pulling of the arm.

• Presentation: Child holds arm slightly flexed and pronated; refuses to use the arm.
• Management: Reduction via Hyperpronation or Supination-Flexion. No X-rays needed if the history is clear.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the child starts using the arm normally immediately after the maneuver, they can be discharged. No sling or follow-up is necessary.

quiz.q: What is the maneuver used to reduce a nursemaid's elbow?

title: 🦋 PRIMARY HYPERPARATHYROIDISM
content:
Usually due to a single parathyroid adenoma.

• Labs: High Calcium, High PTH, Low Phosphorus.
• Symptoms: 'Stones, Bones, Groans, and Moans' (Kidney stones, bone pain, constipation, depression).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If Calcium is High and PTH is Normal, this is still inappropriate (PTH should be low in hypercalcemia) and is likely primary hyperparathyroidism.

quiz.q: What is the classic lab finding (Calcium/PTH/Phos) in primary hyperparathyroidism?
quiz.a: High Calcium, High PTH, Low Phosphorus.

title: 🚗 REPORTING TO MINISTRY OF TRANSPORT
content:
A patient with newly diagnosed Epilepsy or a recent Stroke with cognitive deficits.

• Action: In most Canadian provinces (like Ontario), the physician is legally mandated to report the patient to the Ministry of Transportation (MOT).
• Note: You must inform the patient of your duty to report.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Patient confidentiality is overridden by the duty to protect public safety in these specific reporting scenarios.

quiz.q: Is a physician legally required to report a patient who is unfit to drive to the authorities?
quiz.a: Yes, it is a mandatory reporting requirement in most of Canada.

title: 📋 NOTIFIABLE DISEASES: SYPHILIS
content:
A 22-year-old man tests positive for primary syphilis (RPR + FTA-ABS).

• Next Best Step: Report to Public Health and screen for other STIs (HIV, GC/CT).
• Tx: Penicillin G Benzathine 2.4 million units IM (Single dose).

⚠️ Exam Trap: You must treat the Partners from the last 90 days, even if their test is negative. Public Health will assist with 'Partner Notification'.

quiz.q: What is the first-line treatment for primary syphilis?

title: 🦠 HEPATITIS B SEROLOGY (CHRONIC)
content:
A 32-year-old man has a positive HBsAg for 8 months. Anti-HBs is negative, and Anti-HBc IgG is positive.

• Interpretation: Chronic Hepatitis B infection.
• Next Best Step: Test for HBeAg and HBV DNA levels to assess viral replication and infectivity.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Presence of HBsAg for > 6 months defines chronicity. Anti-HBs is only positive after successful vaccination or recovery from acute infection.

quiz.q: Which marker indicates that a Hepatitis B infection has become chronic?
quiz.a: Persistence of HBsAg for more than 6 months.

title: 👶 PYLORIC STENOSIS (STABILIZATION)
content:
A 4-week-old boy has projectile, non-bilious vomiting. He is lethargic with dry mucous membranes.

• Immediate Next Step: IV Fluid Resuscitation and correction of electrolytes.
• Logic: Pyloric stenosis causes 'Hypokalemic, hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis'. You must stabilize the chemistry before the surgeon can operate (Pyloromyotomy).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT rush to the OR. Operating on a child with uncorrected metabolic alkalosis significantly increases anesthesia risk (apnea).

quiz.q: What is the priority before surgical repair of hypertrophic pyloric stenosis?
quiz.a: Correction of fluid and electrolyte imbalances (alkalosis).

title: ⚖️ MINOR REFUSING TRANSFUSION (QUEBEC)
content:
In Quebec, a 15-year-old Jehovah's Witness is stable but requires a blood transfusion for chronic anemia. He refuses based on religious beliefs.

• Rule: In Quebec, a minor 14+ can consent alone, but refusal of life-saving/necessary treatment can be overridden by a Court Order if the physician believes it's in the minor's best interest.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In an Emergency where the minor's life is in immediate danger, you can transfuse without a court order to save the life.

quiz.q: In Quebec, what is required to override a 16-year-old's refusal of necessary medical treatment?
quiz.a: A court order (authorization from the court).

title: 🧱 RUPTURED AAA (UNSTABLE)
content:
A 70-year-old man has sudden severe back pain and a BP of 85/50. He has a known 5.0 cm AAA.

• Next Best Step: Immediate Transfer to the Operating Room (OR) for emergency repair.
• Note: If the patient is Unstable, do NOT send them for a CT scan. The diagnosis is clinical/bedside ultrasound.

⚠️ Exam Trap: CT Angiography is only for Stable patients to plan surgery. For an unstable patient with the classic triad (pain, hypotension, pulsatile mass), the OR is the only destination.

quiz.q: What is the destination for a hemodynamically unstable patient with a suspected ruptured AAA?
quiz.a: The Operating Room (for immediate surgical or endovascular repair).

title: 🦴 GIANT CELL ARTERITIS (GCA)
content:
A 68-year-old woman has a new-onset headache, jaw claudication, and sudden blurring of vision in one eye.

• Immediate Next Step: High-dose Systemic Corticosteroids (Prednisone).
• Note: Do NOT wait for the temporal artery biopsy results. Preventing permanent blindness in the other eye is the absolute priority.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Biopsy should be done within 1-2 weeks of starting steroids, but treatment must start immediately upon clinical suspicion.

quiz.q: What is the priority treatment for suspected Giant Cell Arteritis with visual symptoms?

title: 🤰 SHOULDER DYSTOCIA (ALARM)
content:
During delivery, the fetal head is delivered but the shoulders are stuck (Turtle sign).

• First Step: Call for help and perform McRoberts Maneuver (hyperflexion of maternal thighs) + Suprapubic Pressure.

⚠️ Exam Trap: NEVER apply fundal pressure; it will further impact the shoulder and risk uterine rupture. Episiotomy may be done to provide more room for maneuvers, but it doesn't resolve the bone-on-bone impaction.

quiz.q: What is the initial maneuver for managing shoulder dystocia?
quiz.a: McRoberts maneuver (often combined with suprapubic pressure).

title: 🧠 LITHIUM TOXICITY (DRUG INTERACTIONS)
content:
A patient on Lithium for bipolar disorder is started on a new medication for hypertension and develops ataxia, coarse tremors, and confusion.

• Likely Culprit: Thiazide Diuretics, NSAIDs, or ACE inhibitors.
• Mechanism: These drugs reduce the renal clearance of Lithium, leading to toxic levels.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient on Lithium needs an analgesic, Acetaminophen is safe. Avoid Ibuprofen/Naproxen as they consistently raise Lithium levels.

quiz.q: Which common antihypertensive class is most known for causing Lithium toxicity?

title: 🌬️ COPD EXACERBATION (ANTIBIOTICS)
content:
A 65-year-old with COPD has increased dyspnea and cough.

• Indications for Antibiotics: Increased Dyspnea, Increased Sputum Volume, AND Increased Sputum Purulence (The Anthonisen Criteria).
• Note: You only need 2 out of 3 if one of them is purulence.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Not every COPD flare needs antibiotics. If the sputum is clear and volume hasn't changed, focus on bronchodilators and steroids.

quiz.q: What is the key sputum change that justifies starting antibiotics in a COPD exacerbation?

title: 🗣️ THE ANGRY PATIENT
content:
A patient is shouting at the receptionist because their appointment is 45 minutes late.

• Best Approach: Validate their feelings and apologize for the delay. 'I can see you're frustrated, and I'm sorry you've had to wait.'
• Principle: Empathy and de-escalation are the first steps in professional communication.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT explain 'why' you are late or tell the patient they are being 'unreasonable' until you have first acknowledged their feelings.

quiz.q: What is the first step in managing an encounter with a frustrated or angry patient?
quiz.a: Acknowledge and validate the patient’s feelings (empathy).

title: 🔪 HEMORRHOIDS: INTERNAL VS EXTERNAL
content:
FeatureInternalExternal
LocationAbove dentate lineBelow dentate line
PainPainless (usually)Painful (if thrombosed)
ManagementBanding / SclerotherapyExcision (if acute/painful)

⚠️ Exam Trap: Internal hemorrhoids present with bright red blood per rectum. If they prolapse and cannot be reduced, they may become painful (strangulated). Initial treatment for both is always High-fiber diet and sitz baths.

quiz.q: Which type of hemorrhoids are characteristically painless but cause rectal bleeding?

title: 👶 INTUSSUSCEPTION (NEXT STEP)
content:
A 10-month-old has intermittent screaming and 'currant jelly' stools.

• Best Initial Test: Ultrasound (Target/Donut sign).
• Next Best Step (Treatment): Air (Pneumatic) Enema.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Air enema is both diagnostic and therapeutic. Only go to surgery if the enema fails or if the child has signs of Peritonitis (rigid abdomen).

quiz.q: What is the preferred non-surgical method for reducing an intussusception?

title: 📊 CASE-CONTROL VS COHORT
content:
Study TypeDirectionMeasure
Case-ControlRetrospective (Look back)Odds Ratio (OR)
CohortProspective (Look forward)Relative Risk (RR)

⚠️ Exam Trap: Case-control studies are great for Rare Diseases. Cohort studies are better for Rare Exposures. Only a Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT) can prove causation.

quiz.q: Which study design is most appropriate for studying a very rare disease?

title: 🦴 SLE: DIAGNOSTIC STEPS
content:
A 24-year-old woman has joint pain, a malar rash, and photosensitivity.

• Best Initial Test: ANA (Antinuclear Antibody) - High sensitivity, but low specificity.
• Most Specific Tests: Anti-dsDNA and Anti-Smith.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Anti-dsDNA levels often correlate with Disease Activity (especially lupus nephritis), while Anti-Smith remains constant.

quiz.q: Which autoantibody is most specific for Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)?

title: 🤰 METHOTREXATE CRITERIA
content:
A patient has a confirmed stable ectopic pregnancy.

• Criteria for Methotrexate (MTX):
1. Hemodynamically stable.
2. β-hCG < 5000 IU/L.
3. Fetal cardiac activity absent.
4. Mass size < 3.5 - 4 cm.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has Peritoneal signs (rebound/guarding) or is hypotensive, she needs Emergency Surgery (Salpingectomy), not MTX.

quiz.q: What is the maximum β-hCG level generally considered safe for treatment with Methotrexate?

title: 🧠 POSITIVE VS NEGATIVE SYMPTOMS
content:
CategorySymptomsTreatment Response
PositiveHallucinations, Delusions, Disorganized speechGood (Antipsychotics)
NegativeApathy, Social withdrawal, Flat affect (6 A's)Poor

⚠️ Exam Trap: Negative symptoms are often more disabling and harder to treat. Second-generation (atypical) antipsychotics are slightly better for negative symptoms than first-generation.

quiz.q: Which type of symptoms in schizophrenia respond best to dopaminergic blockade?

title: 🌬️ ACUTE ASTHMA (SEVERITY)
content:
A 15-year-old in the ED with an asthma flare. He is unable to speak in full sentences.

• Best indicator of severity: Peak Expiratory Flow (PEF) or FEV1.
• Danger Sign: A 'Normal' PaCO2 on ABG. In a severe flare, the patient should be hyperventilating (Low CO2). A normal or high CO2 means the patient is tiring out and may need intubation.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT wait for a chest X-ray to start treatment (Oxygen, SABA, Ipratropium, Corticosteroids).

quiz.q: What does a 'normal' PaCO2 in an acutely breathless asthma patient indicate?

title: ⚖️ CAPACITY: SPECIFICITY RULE
content:
A 90-year-old with mild dementia wants to change his will.

• Rule: Capacity is Task-Specific. A patient may be incapable of managing complex finances but still Capable of deciding where to live or making a simple will.
• Action: Assess the patient's capacity specifically for the task at hand.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A diagnosis of 'Dementia' does not automatically mean 'Incapable'. You must perform a formal assessment for the specific decision being made.

quiz.q: Is a patient with a diagnosis of dementia automatically considered incapable of making medical decisions?
quiz.a: No, capacity must be assessed for each specific decision.

title: 🤰 APPENDICITIS IN PREGNANCY
content:
A pregnant woman (20 weeks) has RLQ pain. Ultrasound is inconclusive.

• Next Best Step: MRI Abdomen (without gadolinium).
• Logic: MRI is the preferred second-line imaging in pregnancy to avoid ionizing radiation (CT).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Remember that in the 3rd trimester, the appendix is pushed upwards toward the RUQ, so the location of pain may change.

quiz.q: What is the preferred second-line imaging for suspected appendicitis in a pregnant patient if ultrasound is negative?

title: 👶 MEASLES (VITAMIN A)
content:
A 4-year-old child from an unvaccinated community is diagnosed with Measles.

• Evidence-Based Adjunct: Vitamin A supplementation.
• Benefit: Reduces the severity of symptoms and the risk of complications (like blindness and pneumonia).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Measles (Rubeola) is highly contagious. The next best public health step is to Isolate the patient and notify Public Health immediately.

quiz.q: Which vitamin supplement is recommended for children with acute measles infection?

title: 🦴 GOUT (ACUTE TX)
content:
A 55-year-old man has a red, swollen, exquisitely tender big toe.

• First-line: NSAIDs (e.g., Indomethacin, Naproxen) or Colchicine.
• If Contraindicated (e.g., Renal failure / Peptic ulcer): Use Corticosteroids (Oral or Intra-articular).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT start Allopurinol during an acute attack. If the patient is already taking it, continue the same dose; do not change it until the flare is gone (2 weeks later).

quiz.q: What is the preferred first-line treatment for an acute gout flare in a patient with no contraindications?

title: 🦠 MENINGITIS: INITIAL STEPS
content:
A 22-year-old with fever, headache, and neck stiffness. Positive Brudzinski sign.

• Absolute Priority: Start Empiric Antibiotics (Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin) + Dexamethasone.
• Next Best Step (Dx): Lumbar Puncture (LP) (unless signs of high ICP).


⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has altered mental status or focal neuro deficits, perform a CT Head BEFORE the LP to rule out herniation risk. But NEVER delay antibiotics for the CT scan.

quiz.q: When should corticosteroids be administered in suspected bacterial meningitis?
quiz.a: Prior to or along with the first dose of antibiotics.

title: 🩸 ANEMIA OF CHRONIC DISEASE (ACD)
content:
Common in patients with rheumatoid arthritis, CKD, or chronic infections.

• Labs: Low Serum Iron, Low TIBC, and High/Normal Ferritin.
• Mechanism: Hepcidin sequestration of iron in macrophages.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Differentiate from Iron Deficiency Anemia (IDA), where TIBC is High and Ferritin is Low. Ferritin is an acute-phase reactant, so it's always high in inflammation.

quiz.q: What is the typical Ferritin level in Anemia of Chronic Disease?

title: 🧠 ADHD: DIAGNOSIS & TX
content:
Symptoms of inattention and/or hyperactivity in ≥ 2 settings (home/school) for ≥ 6 months.

• First-line (School-age): Stimulants (Methylphenidate / Amphetamines).
• Side Effects: Insomnia, weight loss (appetite suppression).

⚠️ Exam Trap: For Preschoolers (age 4-5), the first-line treatment is Behavioral Therapy, not medications.

quiz.q: What is the pharmacological first-line treatment for ADHD in children aged 6 and older?

title: 🤱 POSTPARTUM DEPRESSION (PPD)
content:
Symptoms of depression starting within 4 weeks of delivery (up to 1 year).

• Screening: Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS).
• Management: Psychotherapy for mild cases; SSRIs (Sertraline) for moderate/severe cases.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Distinguish from 'Postpartum Blues', which peaks at Day 5 and resolves by Day 10. Blues does NOT cause functional impairment or suicidal ideation.

quiz.q: How do you distinguish Postpartum Blues from Postpartum Depression?
quiz.a: Blues is self-limiting (resolves within 2 weeks); PPD is persistent and more severe.

title: 🦋 DIABETES: SCREENING (CANADA)
content:
Canadian (Diabetes Canada) guidelines for average-risk adults.

• Who: Everyone ≥ 40 years old.
• How: HbA1c (≥ 6.5%) or Fasting Plasma Glucose (≥ 7.0 mmol/L).
• Frequency: Every 3 years.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You must Repeat the test (unless patient has clear symptoms of hyperglycemia) to confirm the diagnosis of Type 2 Diabetes.

quiz.q: What is the diagnostic threshold for HbA1c in Diabetes Mellitus?

title: 🦵 PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (ABI)
content:
Intermittent claudication (pain with walking, relieved by rest).

• Best Initial Test: Ankle-Brachial Index (ABI).
• Interpretation: ABI ≤ 0.90 is diagnostic for PAD.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the ABI is > 1.4, it means the vessels are Non-compressible (calcified), often seen in Diabetics. In this case, use Toe-Brachial Index (TBI).

quiz.q: What ABI value is considered diagnostic for Peripheral Artery Disease?

title: 🍺 ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL (PHASES)
content:
TimeSymptoms
6 - 12hTremor, anxiety, palpitations.
12 - 48hSeizures / Alcoholic Hallucinosis (stable vitals).
48 - 96hDelirium Tremens (DTs): Confusion, Agitation, Autonomic Instability.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Treatment for DTs is high-dose Benzodiazepines (Diazepam or Lorazepam). In patients with Liver failure, use 'LOT' (Lorazepam, Oxazepam, Temazepam) to avoid metabolism issues.

quiz.q: When does Delirium Tremens (DTs) typically occur after the last drink?

title: ⚖️ CONSENT: THE UNCONSCIOUS PATIENT
content:
An unconscious patient arrives at the ED with a life-threatening injury. No family is present.

• Rule: Emergency Privilege. Consent is Presumed for life-saving or limb-saving treatment.
• Action: Proceed with necessary treatment immediately.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If you find a 'No Blood' card (Jehovah's Witness) or a DNR order in their wallet, you MUST respect it, as it represents the patient's prior capable wish.

quiz.q: Under what legal doctrine can a physician treat an unconscious patient in an emergency without consent?

title: 🧱 CELIAC DISEASE (DX)
content:
Autoimmune reaction to gluten causing malabsorption.

• Initial Step: IgA Anti-tissue Transglutaminase (tTG) antibody.
• Definitive Test: Small bowel Biopsy (showing villous atrophy).

⚠️ Exam Trap: The patient MUST be eating gluten for these tests to be accurate. If they have started a gluten-free diet, the tests may be false negative. Also, check total IgA level to rule out IgA deficiency.
