quiz.q: What is the initial screening test of choice for Celiac Disease?

title: 👶 PERTUSSIS (WHOOPING COUGH)
content:
Catarrhal stage (1-2w) → Paroxysmal stage (2-6w) with inspiratory 'whoop' and post-tussive emesis.

• Dx: Nasopharyngeal swab for PCR (most sensitive).
• Tx: Macrolides (Azithromycin). Treat all household contacts regardless of vaccination status.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Infants, Pertussis may present with Apnea rather than a classic cough. This is a medical emergency.

quiz.q: What is the preferred antibiotic for both treatment and post-exposure prophylaxis of Pertussis?

title: 🧠 MULTIPLE SCLEROSIS (DX)
content:
Demyelinating disease separated in 'Time and Space'.

• Best Initial/Definitive Imaging: MRI with Gadolinium (shows white matter plaques).
• CSF Finding: Oligoclonal Bands (if MRI is inconclusive).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Optic Neuritis (painful vision loss) is a common first presentation. Treatment for acute flares is High-dose IV Methylprednisolone.

quiz.q: What is the classic finding on CSF analysis in patients with Multiple Sclerosis?

title: 🤰 PREECLAMPSIA: PPX
content:
A pregnant woman has risk factors for preeclampsia (e.g., prior preeclampsia, twin pregnancy, chronic HTN).

• Prevention: Low-dose Aspirin (81-162 mg) started between 12 and 16 weeks of gestation.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Aspirin should be continued until delivery. It is the only medication proven to reduce the incidence of preeclampsia in high-risk women.

quiz.q: When should low-dose aspirin be started for preeclampsia prophylaxis in high-risk women?

title: 🫀 ATRIAL FIBRILLATION (CHADS-65)
content:
Determining the need for anticoagulation in Canada.

• Start Oral Anticoagulant (OAC) IF:
1. Age ≥ 65 OR
2. Prior Stroke/TIA OR
3. CHADS criteria (CHF, HTN, DM).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canada, we use CHADS-65. If the patient is < 65 and has none of the risk factors, they do NOT need Aspirin or OAC. OAC (DOACs like Apixaban) is preferred over Warfarin.

quiz.q: According to Canadian guidelines, what is the age threshold for starting anticoagulation in Atrial Fibrillation?

title: 🧱 DIVERTICULITIS: TX
content:
SeverityManagement
UncomplicatedOutpatient: Clear fluids + Oral Antibiotics.
Complicated (Abscess/Perforation)Admission, NPO, IV Fluids, IV Antibiotics.

⚠️ Exam Trap: For a large abscess (> 3-4 cm), the next best step is CT-guided Percutaneous Drainage. Only go to surgery for generalized peritonitis or failure of medical therapy.

quiz.q: What is the best imaging modality for diagnosing acute diverticulitis?

title: 🦋 DIABETIC KETOACIDOSIS (TX)
content:
The 3 Pillars of Treatment:
1. Fluids: Aggressive Normal Saline (0.9% NS).
2. Insulin: IV infusion (only AFTER K+ is > 3.3).
3. Potassium: Mandatory replacement as insulin will drive K+ into cells.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Bicarbonate is almost NEVER used unless pH is < 6.9. The main goal is to close the Anion Gap.

quiz.q: What is the most important initial step in the management of DKA?

title: 🚽 NEPHROLITHIASIS (KIDNEY STONES)
content:
A patient with sudden onset colicky flank pain and hematuria.

• Best Initial Test: Non-contrast CT Abdomen/Pelvis (Stone Protocol).
• Tx (< 5mm): Fluids, analgesia (NSAIDs), and Tamsulosin (to help passage).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the stone is associated with Fever/Infection (Urosepsis) or AKI, it is a surgical emergency. The next best step is Urgent Decompression (Nephrostomy tube or Stent).

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive imaging for detecting kidney stones?

title: ⚖️ FITNESS TO DRIVE (DEMENTIA)
content:
A patient is diagnosed with Moderate Dementia and is still driving.

• Physician's Role: You have a Legal Duty to Report the patient to the Ministry of Transportation (in most provinces).
• Action: Inform the patient/family and send the report. This overrides confidentiality.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You do NOT need the patient's consent to report them if they are medically unfit to drive and pose a risk to public safety.

quiz.q: Is a physician legally required to report a patient with cognitive impairment that affects driving safety?

title: 🌬️ OBSTRUCTIVE SLEEP APNEA (OSA)
content:
Daytime sleepiness, snoring, and witnessed apneas.

• Dx: Polysomnography (Sleep Study).
• Management: CPAP is the gold standard for moderate/severe cases.

⚠️ Exam Trap: OSA is a major risk factor for Hypertension, AFib, and Heart Failure. Always screen obese patients with HTN for OSA.

quiz.q: What is the definitive diagnostic test for Obstructive Sleep Apnea?

title: 🧠 LITHIUM: MONITORING
content:
Before starting Lithium, you must obtain Baseline Tests.

• Tests: Creatinine (Renal function), TSH (Thyroid function), Calcium, and β-hCG (Pregnancy).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Lithium can cause Diabetes Insipidus (nephrogenic) and Hypothyroidism. Monitor levels every 3-6 months once stable.

quiz.q: Which two organ systems must be monitored regularly in a patient on Lithium?

title: 🚑 TENSION PNEUMOTHORAX
content:
Hypotension, jugular venous distension, and absent breath sounds.

• Immediate Next Step: Needle Decompression (2nd intercostal space, mid-clavicular line) or Finger Thoracostomy.
• Followed by: Chest Tube insertion.

⚠️ Exam Trap: This is a Clinical Diagnosis. Do NOT wait for an X-ray if the patient is unstable; you must decompress immediately to save their life.

quiz.q: What is the definitive initial treatment for tension pneumothorax in an unstable patient?

title: 🏭 ASBESTOSIS & LUNG CANCER
content:
A 60-year-old former construction worker presents with progressive dyspnea and a dry cough. Imaging shows pleural plaques.

• Key Fact: Asbestos exposure increases the risk of both Lung Cancer and Mesothelioma.
• Next Best Step: Smoking Cessation (Smoking acts synergistically with asbestos to massively increase lung cancer risk).

⚠️ Exam Trap: While Mesothelioma is the most specific cancer for asbestos, Bronchogenic Carcinoma (Lung Cancer) is actually the most common cancer in these patients.

quiz.q: Which cancer is most commonly associated with asbestos exposure in a smoker?

title: 👶 HAND, FOOT, AND MOUTH DISEASE
content:
A 3-year-old has painful oral ulcers and a vesicular rash on the palms and soles. He has a low-grade fever.

• Etiology: Coxsackievirus A16.
• Management: Supportive Care (Hydration and analgesia). It is self-limiting.


⚠️ Exam Trap: The child is contagious while the vesicles are present, but there is no need for prolonged school exclusion once the fever is gone and the child feels well.

quiz.q: What is the most common viral cause of Hand, Foot, and Mouth Disease?

title: 🧱 CIRRHOSIS: SCREENING FOR HCC
content:
A 55-year-old man with chronic Hepatitis C and confirmed cirrhosis comes for follow-up.

• Recommended Screening: Ultrasound of the Liver every 6 months.
• Goal: Early detection of Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is no longer recommended as a standalone screening tool due to low specificity. It can be used adjunctive to ultrasound.

quiz.q: How often should a patient with cirrhosis be screened for Hepatocellular Carcinoma?

title: 🤰 PREMATURE RUPTURE OF MEMBRANES (PROM)
content:
A 39-week pregnant woman has a 'gush of fluid'. Nitrazine test is blue, and Ferning is positive. No contractions.

• Next Best Step: Induction of Labor (usually with Oxytocin).
• Logic: At term, inducing labor reduces the risk of Chorioamnionitis compared to expectant management.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Avoid frequent Digital Vaginal Exams once membranes have ruptured to prevent ascending infection. Use sterile speculum exams only.

quiz.q: What is the preferred management for term PROM (≥ 37 weeks) without contractions?

title: 🧠 SOMATIC SYMPTOM DISORDER
content:
A 30-year-old woman has multiple physical complaints (pain, fatigue, bloating) for years. All medical tests are normal. She is extremely distressed by the symptoms.

• Best Management: Regularly scheduled visits with a single primary care physician.
• Goal: Avoid unnecessary tests/specialist referrals and address the psychosocial distress.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT tell the patient 'it's all in your head'. Acknowledge that the symptoms are real to the patient, but focus on functional improvement rather than 'curing' the symptom.

quiz.q: What is the cornerstone of managing Somatic Symptom Disorder?
quiz.a: Regularly scheduled appointments with a primary physician to limit 'doctor shopping' and unnecessary testing.

title: 🩸 WARFARIN OVERDOSE (MANAGEMENT)
content:
INR LevelBleeding?Action
< 5.0NoLower or omit dose
5.0 - 9.0NoOmit dose + Oral Vitamin K (1-2mg)
> 9.0NoOmit dose + Oral Vitamin K (2.5-5mg)
Any INRMajor BleedPCC (Prothrombin Complex) + IV Vit K

⚠️ Exam Trap: For serious bleeding, use PCC instead of FFP if available, as it works faster and doesn't require volume overload.

quiz.q: What is the first-line treatment for life-threatening bleeding in a patient on Warfarin?
quiz.a: Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (PCC) and IV Vitamin K.

title: 🧱 GALLSTONE PANCREATITIS
content:
A patient has acute pancreatitis. Ultrasound shows stones in the gallbladder and a dilated Common Bile Duct (CBD). Jaundice is present.

• Next Best Step: ERCP (to remove the obstructing stone).
• Follow-up: Cholecystectomy should be performed during the same admission once the inflammation subsides.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has pancreatitis but NO signs of CBD obstruction (normal bilirubin/normal duct size), ERCP is NOT indicated. Focus on aggressive IV fluids.

quiz.q: When is ERCP indicated in the management of acute pancreatitis?
quiz.a: When there is concurrent cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction (choledocholithiasis).

title: 🧠 AUTISM SPECTRUM DISORDER (ASD)
content:
A 2-year-old does not make eye contact, has delayed speech, and performs repetitive hand-flapping.

• Next Best Step: Refer for Comprehensive Developmental Assessment and Audiology (hearing test).
• Logic: You must rule out hearing loss in any child with speech delay before diagnosing ASD.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Early intervention (Applied Behavior Analysis - ABA) is critical for improving long-term outcomes.

quiz.q: What test must be ordered for any child presenting with a delay in language or social skills?

title: ⚖️ CHILD ABUSE (REPORTING)
content:
You suspect child abuse in a 5-year-old with multiple bruises of different ages. The parents provide a vague explanation.

• Action: Immediately report to Child Protective Services (CPS) or the police.
• Principle: Reporting is mandatory and does not require proof—only 'reasonable suspicion'.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You do NOT need the parents' consent to report, and you should not 'investigate' the case yourself. Reporting is your only legal and ethical duty.

quiz.q: What is the legal requirement for a physician who suspects child abuse?
quiz.a: Mandatory reporting to child welfare authorities (e.g., CAS in Ontario).

title: 🦋 HYPERTHYROIDISM: SUBACUTE THYROIDITIS
content:
A patient has a painful, tender thyroid gland following a viral upper respiratory infection. TSH is low, T4 is high.

• Key Finding: Low Iodine Uptake on Radioactive Iodine Scan (RAIU).
• Treatment: NSAIDs or steroids for pain; Beta-blockers for symptoms. Antithyroid drugs (PTU/Methimazole) are not used.

⚠️ Exam Trap: This is a 'leak' of pre-formed hormone, not overproduction. It usually resolves spontaneously after a brief hypothyroid phase.

quiz.q: What is the characteristic finding on a radioactive iodine scan in subacute (De Quervain) thyroiditis?

title: 🧴 HERPES ZOSTER (SHINGLES)
content:
Painful, vesicular rash in a single dermatome that does not cross the midline.

• Treatment: Oral Acyclovir/Valacyclovir within 72 hours of onset.
• Complication: Post-herpetic neuralgia (Tx: Gabapentin/Amitriptyline).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the rash involves the tip of the nose (Hutchinson sign), the next best step is Urgent Ophthalmology Referral to rule out Herpes Zoster Ophthalmicus.

quiz.q: What is the time window for starting antiviral therapy in shingles to be most effective?

title: 🫀 STABLE ANGINA (INVESTIGATION)
content:
A 55-year-old has exertional chest pain relieved by rest. ECG is normal.

• Next Best Step: Exercise Stress Test (if ECG is normal and patient can exercise).
• If ECG is abnormal (e.g., LBBB): Stress Imaging (Nuclear or Echo) is required.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A negative stress test does not rule out CAD completely, but it helps risk-stratify the patient for further invasive testing (Angiography).

quiz.q: What is the first-line investigation for a patient with stable angina and a normal resting ECG?

title: 👶 BACTERIAL TRACHEITIS
content:
A child presents like Croup but looks much sicker (toxic), has high fever, and does not respond to nebulized epinephrine.

• Etiology: S. aureus.
• Management: Airway Stabilization (Intubation often required) and IV Antibiotics.

⚠️ Exam Trap: This is a rare but life-threatening emergency. Differentiate from Epiglottitis (where there is drooling and no 'barky' cough). In Tracheitis, there is often purulent secretions.

quiz.q: What is the most common causative organism of bacterial tracheitis?

title: 🦋 DIABETES: STATIN INDICATIONS (CANADA)
content:
According to Diabetes Canada, a Statin should be started in any Diabetic patient who is:

1. Age ≥ 40 years.
2. Age < 40 with macrovascular disease, microvascular disease, or long duration of DM (> 15y).

⚠️ Exam Trap: The target LDL for these high-risk patients is < 2.0 mmol/L or a > 50% reduction from baseline.

quiz.q: At what age should a patient with Diabetes Mellitus routinely start a statin regardless of their LDL level?

title: 🤰 GBS SCREENING (CANADA)
content:
Group B Streptococcus (GBS) screening during pregnancy.

• Timing: 35 - 37 weeks gestation via vaginal-anorectal swab.
• Intrapartum Prophylaxis: IV Penicillin G during labor if GBS positive.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You do NOT need to screen if the patient has GBS bacteriuria during the current pregnancy or had a previous infant with GBS disease; these patients get prophylaxis automatically.

quiz.q: When is Group B Strep (GBS) screening performed in pregnancy?

title: 🔪 CHOLEDOCHOLITHIASIS (MANAGEMENT)
content:
Common Bile Duct (CBD) stone. Presents with RUQ pain and Jaundice (High Bilirubin/ALP).

• Best Initial Test: Ultrasound (shows dilated CBD).
• Gold Standard / Tx: ERCP for stone extraction.

⚠️ Exam Trap: MRCP is a non-invasive diagnostic tool used if the suspicion is moderate, but ERCP is the 'Next Step' if the suspicion is high or if the patient is jaundiced.

quiz.q: What is the definitive procedure to remove a stone from the common bile duct?

title: ⚖️ BOUNDARIES: GIFTS FROM PATIENTS
content:
A patient offers you a $500 gift certificate as a 'thank you' for a successful surgery.

• General Rule: Small, inexpensive, or hand-made gifts may be accepted. Expensive or monetary gifts should be declined politely.
• Reason: Large gifts can create a sense of obligation or a 'special' relationship that compromises professional boundaries.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Always acknowledge the patient's gratitude while explaining that professional standards prevent you from accepting high-value gifts.

quiz.q: How should a physician respond to a high-value monetary gift from a patient?
quiz.a: Politely decline the gift to maintain professional boundaries.

title: 🧠 STATUS EPILEPTICUS (ALGORITHM)
content:
A seizure lasting > 5 minutes.

• Step 1 (0-5 min): Benzodiazepines (IV Lorazepam or IM Midazolam).
• Step 2 (5-20 min): Phenytoin, Levetiracetam, or Valproate.
• Step 3 (> 20 min): Intubation and Propofol or Phenobarbital.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT wait for an EEG to start treatment. Secure the airway and give the Benzo immediately.

quiz.q: What is the first-line pharmacotherapy for status epilepticus?

title: 👶 PERTUSSIS (CONTACTS)
content:
A 2-month-old is diagnosed with Pertussis. He has a 4-year-old sister who is up-to-date on her vaccines.

• Action for the sister: Prophylaxis with Azithromycin for 5 days.
• Logic: Prophylaxis is required for all household contacts regardless of their immunization status to prevent transmission to vulnerable infants.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Immunization reduces the severity of disease but does not completely prevent transmission/colonization.

quiz.q: Should a fully vaccinated sibling of a child with pertussis receive post-exposure prophylaxis?
quiz.a: Yes, macrolide prophylaxis is recommended for all household contacts.

title: 🔥 TOP 5 SURGICAL NEXT STEPS
content:
1. Acute Cholecystitis: US → Lap Chole.
2. SBO: X-ray → NPO/NG Tube.
3. Appendicitis (Male): Clinical Dx → Surgery.
4. Appendicitis (Female): US/CT → Surgery.
5. Bowel Perforation: X-ray (Air under diaphragm) → Emergency Laparotomy.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Always remember: Unstable patient + Positive FAST = Operating Room. Never the CT scan!

quiz.q: What is the next best step for a patient with free air under the diaphragm on an upright X-ray?
quiz.a: Immediate surgical consultation for exploratory laparotomy.

title: 🧠 PTSD VS ACUTE STRESS DISORDER
content:
A patient experiences a traumatic event (e.g., car accident) and has flashbacks and avoidance.

• Acute Stress Disorder: Symptoms last 3 days to 1 month.
• PTSD: Symptoms last > 1 month.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Treatment for both starts with Trauma-focused CBT. SSRIs are only first-line for PTSD if therapy is not sufficient or unavailable.

quiz.q: At what duration of symptoms does Acute Stress Disorder become PTSD?

title: 🦠 C. DIFFICILE INFECTION
content:
Profuse watery diarrhea after recent antibiotic use (Clindamycin, Fluoroquinolones).

• First-line Treatment: Oral Vancomycin or Fidaxomicin.
• Management: Discontinue the offending antibiotic and use contact precautions.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Metronidazole is no longer the first-line choice for C. diff in Canada for any severity level. Always go with Oral Vancomycin.

quiz.q: What is the first-line oral antibiotic for Clostridioides difficile infection?

title: 🎗️ PCOS (ROTTERDAM CRITERIA)
content:
A woman presents with irregular periods, acne, and excess facial hair.

The Triad (Need 2 of 3):
1. Oligo- or Anovulation.
2. Clinical or biochemical Hyperandrogenism.
3. Polycystic ovaries on Ultrasound.

⚠️ Exam Trap: These patients have a high risk of Endometrial Cancer due to unopposed estrogen. Treatment for hirsutism is OCPs; for weight loss, lifestyle is priority.

quiz.q: Why are women with PCOS at an increased risk of endometrial hyperplasia/cancer?
quiz.a: Due to chronic anovulation and unopposed estrogen exposure.

title: 👶 TURNER SYNDROME (45, X)
content:
A 14-year-old girl with short stature, webbed neck, and no breast development.

• Classic Clue: Bicuspid Aortic Valve or Coarctation of the Aorta.
• Labs: High FSH/LH (Primary ovarian failure).


⚠️ Exam Trap: Horseshoe Kidney is also common. Always check blood pressure in both arms and legs to rule out Coarctation.

quiz.q: What is the most common cardiac anomaly associated with Turner Syndrome?

title: 🧠 SPINAL CORD COMPRESSION (ONCOLOGY)
content:
Oncology Emergency! A patient with history of cancer has new back pain and leg weakness.

• Immediate Next Step: IV Dexamethasone (High-dose).
• Next Step (Imaging): Emergent MRI of the entire spine.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT wait for the MRI to give steroids. Reducing cord edema is the priority to prevent permanent paralysis.

quiz.q: What is the priority medical intervention in suspected malignant spinal cord compression?

title: ⚖️ BPPV VS MENIERE’S VS LABYRINTHITIS
content:
ConditionVertigo DurationHearing Loss?
BPPVSeconds (< 1 min)No
Meniere's20 mins - HoursYes (Fluctuating)
LabyrinthitisDays (Continuous)Yes (Sudden)


⚠️ Exam Trap: BPPV is triggered by head movement (Dix-Hallpike (+)). Vestibular Neuritis is like Labyrinthitis but has No hearing loss.

quiz.q: Which cause of vertigo presents with sudden hearing loss and continuous vertigo for several days?

title: 🫀 HYPERKALEMIA: TREATMENT ORDER
content:
ECG shows peaked T-waves or widened QRS.

• Step 1: Calcium Gluconate (to stabilize the heart).
• Step 2: Insulin + Glucose (to shift K+ into cells).
• Step 3: Kayexalate or Dialysis (to remove K+ from the body).


⚠️ Exam Trap: Calcium does NOT lower the potassium level; it only protects the heart. If you give Insulin first without Calcium in a patient with QRS widening, they might go into VFib.

quiz.q: What is the first-line medication for cardiac protection in severe hyperkalemia?

title: 🔥 PARKLAND FORMULA
content:
Fluid resuscitation in major burns.

Formula: 4 mL × % TBSA × Weight (kg)
• Give half in the first 8 hours.
• Give the other half over the next 16 hours.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canada, the target urine output (UOP) is the most accurate indicator of resuscitation: 0.5 mL/kg/hr in adults and 1.0 mL/kg/hr in children.

quiz.q: How much of the total Parkland fluid should be administered in the first 8 hours post-burn?

title: 👁️ DIABETIC RETINOPATHY SCREENING
content:
Type of DiabetesInitial ScreenFollow-up
Type 15 years after diagnosisAnnual
Type 2At time of diagnosisAnnual


⚠️ Exam Trap: In pregnancy, screening should be done in each trimester as pregnancy can rapidly accelerate retinopathy.

quiz.q: When should a patient with newly diagnosed Type 2 Diabetes have their first eye exam?

title: 🧠 MYASTHENIA GRAVIS (TX)
content:
Autoimmune destruction of Post-synaptic Ach receptors.

• Symptoms: Fatigable ptosis, diplopia, and proximal weakness (worse at end of day).
• Initial Treatment: Pyridostigmine (Acetylcholinesterase inhibitor).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Always check for a Thymoma (CT Chest). A Myasthenic Crisis requires IVIG or Plasmapheresis.

quiz.q: What is the first-line symptomatic treatment for Myasthenia Gravis?

title: 🧴 TINEA VERSICOLOR
content:
Hypopigmented, hyperpigmented, or erythematous macules on the trunk.

• Etiology: Malassezia furfur.
• Diagnosis: KOH prep showing 'Spaghetti and Meatballs' (hyphae and spores).
• Tx: Topical Ketoconazole or Selenium Sulfide.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The hypopigmentation can last for months after the fungus is gone. Reassure the patient that the color will eventually return with sun exposure.

quiz.q: What is the characteristic appearance of Malassezia furfur on a KOH preparation?

title: 🩸 HIT (HEPARIN-INDUCED THROMBOCYTOPENIA)
content:
A 5-10 day drop in platelets (> 50%) after starting Heparin.

• Next Best Step: Stop ALL Heparin (even in flushes).
• Treatment: Start a non-heparin anticoagulant (Argatroban or Danaparoid).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Paradoxically, HIT is a Hypercoagulable state; these patients clot (DVT/PE) despite low platelets. Do NOT give platelet transfusions.

quiz.q: What is the priority in managing a patient suspected of having HIT?
quiz.a: Discontinue all forms of heparin immediately.

title: 🦴 OTTAWA ANKLE RULES
content:
Deciding when to X-ray an ankle injury.

Get X-ray IF:
1. Bone tenderness at Posterior edge/Tip of lateral or medial malleolus.
2. Inability to bear weight for 4 steps.

⚠️ Exam Trap: These rules are for Ruling Out. If the patient has none of these, the probability of a fracture is < 1%.

quiz.q: According to the Ottawa Ankle Rules, where should you palpate for tenderness to decide on an X-ray for the lateral side?
quiz.a: Posterior edge or tip of the lateral malleolus.

title: 🤰 PLACENTAL ABRUPTION (SIGNS)
content:
A pregnant woman (34 weeks) presents with sudden, painful vaginal bleeding and a tense (rigid) abdomen.

• Signs: Maternal shock, fetal distress, and frequent contractions.
• Dx: Clinical diagnosis (Ultrasound is not sensitive for abruption).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Distinguish from Placenta Previa, which is Painless bleeding. Abruption is a leading cause of DIC in pregnancy.

quiz.q: How do you clinically distinguish placental abruption from placenta previa?
quiz.a: Abruption is painful; Previa is painless.

title: 🦴 SEPTIC ARTHRITIS (DX)
content:
Medical Emergency! Sudden swollen, hot, extremely painful joint.

• Next Best Step: Arthrocentesis (Joint aspiration).
• Result: WBC > 50,000 cells/mm³ (usually > 100,000).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do not wait for X-rays. If the joint is septic, it will be destroyed within hours. Treat with urgent Surgical Drainage (I&D) and IV antibiotics.

quiz.q: What WBC count threshold on synovial fluid analysis is highly suggestive of septic arthritis?

title: ⚖️ BRAIN DEATH (DEFINITION)
content:
A patient with no brainstem reflexes (pupil, gag, corneal) and no spontaneous respirations.

• Legal Status: Brain death is legal death in Canada.
• Next Step: You do NOT need family consent to withdraw mechanical ventilation (though it should be done sensitively).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Ensure the patient is normothermic and has no sedating drugs in their system before performing the Apnea Test.

quiz.q: Is a patient declared 'brain dead' considered legally deceased in Canada?

title: 🦋 THYROID STORM (TX ORDER)
content:
Life-Threatening Emergency! High fever, tachycardia, and agitation.

Correct Sequence:
1. Beta-blocker (Propranolol).
2. Antithyroid drug (PTU or Methimazole).
3. Iodine (1 hour AFTER PTU).
4. Corticosteroids.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You MUST give PTU/Methimazole before Iodine to prevent the iodine from being used to synthesize more hormone (Wolff-Chaikoff effect).

quiz.q: Why must PTU be given before iodine in thyroid storm?
quiz.a: To block the synthesis of new thyroid hormone before providing the iodine substrate.

title: ⚖️ MEDICAL NEGLIGENCE (4 D's)
content:
To prove malpractice, four elements must be met:

1. Duty: Doctor-patient relationship existed.
2. Dereliction: Breach of the 'Standard of Care'.
3. Damage: Patient suffered harm.
4. Direct Cause: The breach caused the damage.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Standard of Care is defined as what a reasonable peer (a doctor with the same level of training) would do in a similar situation.

quiz.q: What are the four 'D's' required to prove medical negligence?
quiz.a: Duty, Dereliction, Damage, and Direct Cause.

title: 👶 MILESTONE RED FLAG: WALKING
content:
A child is brought in because they are not yet walking.

• Age of Concern: 18 months.
• Action: If a child is not walking by 18 months, they require a Referral for developmental assessment.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Regression (loss of any skill) at any age is a major red flag and requires urgent investigation.

quiz.q: At what age is not walking considered a developmental red flag?

title: 📊 NUMBER NEEDED TO TREAT (NNT)
content:
How many patients must be treated for one to benefit?

$$NNT = rac{1}{ARR}$$
• ARR: Absolute Risk Reduction (Experimental Risk - Control Risk).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a drug reduces stroke risk from 10% to 5%, the ARR is 5% (0.05). The NNT = 1 / 0.05 = 20. A lower NNT means a more effective treatment.

quiz.q: How do you calculate NNT from the Absolute Risk Reduction (ARR)?

title: ⚖️ SOCIAL MEDIA & BOUNDARIES
content:
A patient sends you a 'Friend Request' on Facebook or follows your personal Instagram account.

• Next Best Step: Decline the request and discuss professional boundaries at the next visit if necessary.
• Principle: Physicians must maintain a clear distinction between professional and personal lives to protect the Integrity of the doctor-patient relationship.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Even if the patient is 'nice' or 'long-term', accepting a social media request is a boundary violation. Avoid giving medical advice via private messaging.

quiz.q: What is the appropriate response to a patient's friend request on personal social media?
quiz.a: Decline the request to maintain professional boundaries.

title: 🏭 SILICOSIS (EXPOSURE)
content:
A 50-year-old man who worked in Sandblasting or Mining presents with progressive exertional dyspnea. CXR shows 'Eggshell' calcification of hilar nodes.

• Key Fact: Silica exposure significantly increases the risk of Tuberculosis (TB).
• Next Best Step: Perform a TB Skin Test (PPD) or IGRA as part of the initial workup.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Do not confuse with Asbestosis (Lower lobes/Pleural plaques). Silicosis primarily affects the Upper Lobes.

quiz.q: Patients with silicosis are at a significantly increased risk for which infection?

title: 🦋 HYPERGLYCEMIC HYPEROSMOLAR STATE (HHS)
content:
A 75-year-old with Type 2 DM presents with severe dehydration and confusion. Glucose is 45 mmol/L. No ketones in urine.

• Next Best Step: Aggressive IV Fluid Resuscitation (Normal Saline).
• Note: Patients with HHS have a much larger fluid deficit (8-10L) than those with DKA.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Insulin is secondary in HHS. Correcting the extreme dehydration and hyperosmolality with fluids is the priority to prevent cardiovascular collapse.

quiz.q: What is the primary treatment priority in Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar State (HHS)?

title: 👶 EPIGLOTTITIS (MANAGEMENT)
content:
Life-Threatening Emergency! A 4-year-old is drooling, leaning forward (tripod position), and has high fever.

• Immediate Next Step: Airway Stabilization in a controlled environment (OR/ICU) with an anesthesiologist/ENT present.
• Action: Keep the child calm. Do NOT examine the throat with a tongue depressor.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If the diagnosis is clinically obvious (drooling/tripod), do not waste time on an X-ray. Go straight to airway management.

quiz.q: What is the hallmark radiographic finding in acute epiglottitis?
quiz.a: The 'Thumbprint sign' on a lateral neck X-ray.

title: 🧱 CHRONIC COUGH (DIAGNOSTIC STEPS)
content:
A 45-year-old non-smoker has a cough for 10 weeks. CXR is normal.

• Top 3 Causes: 1. GERD, 2. Post-nasal drip (UACS), 3. Asthma.
• Next Best Step: Empiric trial of Nasal Steroids or Antihistamines (for post-nasal drip) OR Spirometry (for cough-variant asthma).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Always check if the patient is taking an ACE inhibitor. If they are, the next best step is to stop the medication and reassess in 4 weeks.

quiz.q: What is a common medication-related cause of a chronic dry cough?

title: 🫀 HEART FAILURE (ACUTE LMNOP)
content:
A patient presents with acute pulmonary edema, crackles to the mid-lungs, and SpO2 88%.

Management (LMNOP):
1. Lasix (Furosemide).
2. Morphine (Rarely used now, but helps with anxiety/preload).
3. Nitroglycerin (Vasodilator - reduce preload).
4. Oxygen (CPAP/BiPAP preferred).
5. Position (Sit upright).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient is Hypotensive, do NOT give Nitroglycerin. Focus on stabilization and inotropes (Dobutamine).

quiz.q: What is the preferred non-invasive ventilation method for acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema?

title: 🪶 THE CIRCLE OF CARE
content:
A concept in Indigenous health that views wellness as a balance of physical, mental, emotional, and spiritual aspects.

• Next Best Step: Incorporate Traditional Healing or Elder consultation if requested by the patient.
• Principle: Respecting the patient's worldview is essential for Cultural Safety.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do not dismiss traditional medicines (e.g., smudging, cedar) as 'unscientific'. The role of the physician is to ensure they don't interfere with medical treatment while supporting the patient's spiritual needs.

quiz.q: Should a physician support an Indigenous patient's request to use traditional healing alongside medical treatment?
quiz.a: Yes, as long as it does not pose a direct harm or conflict with essential care.

title: 💉 MOTIVATIONAL INTERVIEWING
content:
A parent refuses to vaccinate their child due to concerns about 'autism' or 'toxins'.

• Next Best Step: Use Motivational Interviewing (Ask open-ended questions, affirm, and provide evidence-based info with permission).
• Approach: "Help me understand your concerns...".

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT lecture the patient or tell them they are 'wrong'. This builds resistance. The goal is to build a partnership to protect the child.

quiz.q: What is the most effective communication style for addressing vaccine hesitancy?

title: 🤰 PRETERM LABOR (< 34 WEEKS)
content:
A woman at 30 weeks gestation presents with regular contractions and cervical change.

The Trio of Management:
1. Steroids (Betamethasone) - Lung maturity.
2. Tocolytics (Indomethacin/Nifedipine) - To delay labor for 48h to allow steroids to work.
3. Magnesium Sulfate - For fetal Neuroprotection (prevents cerebral palsy).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Tocolytics do NOT prevent preterm birth; they only buy 48 hours for steroids and transfer to a tertiary center.

quiz.q: Why is Magnesium Sulfate given to women in preterm labor before 32 weeks?
quiz.a: For fetal neuroprotection (to reduce the risk of cerebral palsy).

title: 📚 QUALITY IMPROVEMENT (SENTINEL EVENTS)
content:
A Sentinel Event is a patient safety event that results in death, permanent harm, or severe temporary harm.

• Next Best Step: Perform a Root Cause Analysis (RCA).
• Goal: Identify Systemic failures rather than individual blame.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The goal of an RCA is to prevent the event from happening again by changing processes/systems. It is not a disciplinary tool.

quiz.q: What is the primary purpose of a Root Cause Analysis after a major medical error?
quiz.a: To identify systemic factors and prevent recurrence.

title: 👵 FALLS IN THE ELDERLY (NBS)
content:
An 80-year-old woman is brought in after a fall at home. No obvious injuries.

• Next Best Step: Medication Review (Beers Criteria).
• Logic: Polypharmacy (especially Benzos, Antihypertensives, and Anticholinergics) is a leading cause of falls.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Also check orthostatic vitals, vision, and home safety. A fall is rarely 'just a fall' in the elderly; it's a Geriatric Syndrome.

quiz.q: What is a high-yield modifiable risk factor to assess in an elderly patient who has fallen?

title: 🎀 MASTITIS VS ABSCESS
content:
A breastfeeding mother has a painful, red, swollen breast and fever.

• Mastitis: Diffuse redness. Tx: Continue breastfeeding + Antibiotics (Dicloxacillin).
• Abscess: Fluctuant, localized mass. Tx: Incision & Drainage (or Needle Aspiration) + Antibiotics.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT stop breastfeeding. Stopping leads to milk stasis and worsens the infection/abscess. The milk is safe for the baby.

quiz.q: Should a mother with mastitis or a breast abscess continue to breastfeed from the affected side?
quiz.a: Yes, it helps drain the breast and resolve the infection.

title: 🧠 ILLNESS ANXIETY DISORDER
content:
A patient is preoccupied with having a serious disease (e.g., 'I have a brain tumor') despite no or minimal physical symptoms and negative tests.

• Key Feature: High level of anxiety about health, not the symptoms themselves (unlike Somatic Symptom Disorder).
• Tx: Regular primary care visits + CBT.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Formerly known as 'Hypochondriasis'. The goal is to address the Anxiety and avoid 'Reassurance seeking' through excessive testing.

quiz.q: What is the primary focus of anxiety in Illness Anxiety Disorder?
quiz.a: The fear of having a serious, undiagnosed medical condition.

title: 🧱 ASCITES: SAAG CALCULATION
content:
Determining the cause of abdominal fluid.

$$SAAG = Serum~Albumin - Ascitic~Albumin$$
• SAAG ≥ 11: Portal Hypertension (Cirrhosis, Heart Failure).
• SAAG < 11: Non-portal HTN (Malignancy, TB, Nephrotic syndrome).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If SAAG is high, the fluid is a Transudate (pushed out by pressure). If SAAG is low, it is an Exudate (leaking due to inflammation/leaky vessels).

quiz.q: What does a Serum-Ascites Albumin Gradient (SAAG) of 1.5 g/dL suggest?

title: 🧪 VITAMIN D: WHO TO SCREEN?
content:
A healthy 30-year-old woman asks for a Vitamin D level test.

• Next Best Step: Decline the test and recommend Empiric Supplementation (400-1000 IU).
• Note: Routine screening of Vitamin D in asymptomatic, low-risk adults is NOT recommended in Canada.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Only screen high-risk patients (Osteoporosis, Malabsorption, Renal disease). In Canada, 'lack of sun' is not an indication for testing, just for supplementing.

quiz.q: Is routine Vitamin D testing recommended for healthy asymptomatic adults in Canada?

title: ⚖️ THE UNFIT DRIVER (DEMENTIA)
content:
A patient with moderate Alzheimer's is still driving. He refuses to stop.

• Action: Mandatory Reporting to the Ministry of Transportation.
• Principle: Public Safety overrides patient confidentiality in this specific scenario.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You must inform the patient that you are reporting them, but you do NOT need their consent.

quiz.q: What is the physician's legal obligation when a patient with significant cognitive impairment refuses to stop driving?
quiz.a: Mandatory reporting to the provincial transportation authority.

title: 🦠 LATENT TB (MANAGEMENT)
content:
A healthcare worker has a positive PPD (15mm). CXR is normal. No symptoms.

• Next Best Step: Treatment for Latent TB Infection (LTBI).
• Options: Isoniazid (INH) for 9 months OR Rifampin for 4 months.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Before starting INH, you must check Baseline LFTs and monitor for hepatotoxicity. Always co-prescribe Pyridoxine (Vit B6) to prevent peripheral neuropathy.

quiz.q: What medication must be given alongside Isoniazid to prevent peripheral neuropathy?

title: 🔪 GALLBLADDER POLYP
content:
An incidental 6 mm polyp is found on ultrasound for vague dyspepsia.

• Management: Serial Ultrasound in 6-12 months.
• Indications for Cholecystectomy: Size > 10 mm, symptomatic (biliary colic), or associated with gallstones.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Polyps > 20 mm have a high risk of malignancy and require staging before surgery.

quiz.q: At what size is a gallbladder polyp generally considered for surgical resection?

title: 🩸 THROMBOCYTOPENIA: ITP
content:
Isolated low platelets (< 100) in an otherwise healthy patient. No splenomegaly. Smear shows large platelets.

• Next Best Step: Observation (if Plt > 30 and no bleeding).
• Treatment (if Plt < 30 or bleeding): Corticosteroids or IVIG.

⚠️ Exam Trap: ITP is a Diagnosis of Exclusion. Always rule out HIV and Hepatitis C as they can cause secondary ITP.

quiz.q: What is the first-line medical treatment for Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP)?

title: 🔥 TOP 5 NBS: OBSTETRICS
content:
1. Amenorrhea: hCG test (#1).
2. Pre-eclampsia (High risk): Low-dose Aspirin (12-16w).
3. Non-reactive NST: Stimulate fetus (Vibroacoustic).
4. Active Labor (Red bleed): Bedside Ultrasound (rule out previa).
5. PPH (Atony): Bimanual Uterine Massage + Oxytocin.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Always stabilize the mother (ABCs) before assessing the fetus in any obstetric emergency!

quiz.q: What is the first-line physical maneuver for postpartum hemorrhage due to uterine atony?

title: 👶 PRIMARY AMENORRHEA: TURNER
content:
A 16-year-old girl with short stature, widely spaced nipples, and no breast development has never had a period[span_3](end_span)[span_4](start_span).

• Definitive Investigation: Karyotype[span_4](end_span)[span_5](start_span).
• Logic: The clinical triad (Short stature, spaced nipples, primary amenorrhea) is highly suggestive of Turner Syndrome (45, X)[span_5](end_span)[span_6](start_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: FSH will be high (Primary Ovarian Failure), but the Karyotype is the only way to confirm the chromosomal diagnosis[span_6](end_span).

quiz.q: What is the most definitive investigation for a teenager with short stature and primary amenorrhea?

title: 🗣️ DISCLOSURE & LANGUAGE BARRIERS
content:
A non-English speaking patient has metastatic cancer. [span_7](start_span)[span_8](start_span)Her children ask you NOT to tell her the diagnosis[span_7](end_span)[span_8](end_span)[span_9](start_span)[span_10](start_span).

• Next Best Step: Use an Official Interpreter to ask the patient how much she wants to know[span_9](end_span)[span_10](end_span).
• Principle: Cultural norms do not override the patient's right to Autonomy. [span_11](start_span)The physician must determine the patient's preference for information disclosure directly[span_11](end_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT use family members as interpreters for sensitive news. [span_12](start_span)[span_13](start_span)Do NOT comply with the family's request to hide the truth without asking the patient first[span_12](end_span)[span_13](end_span).

quiz.q: How should a physician handle a family's request to withhold a terminal diagnosis from a patient who doesn't speak English?
quiz.a: Ask the patient through an official interpreter how much information they wish to receive.

title: ⚖️ VACCINE REFUSAL (ETHICS)
content:
Parents of a 2-year-old refuse all vaccinations due to fear of autism, despite thorough counseling[span_14](end_span)[span_15](end_span)[span_16](start_span).

• Next Best Step: Follow the parents' decision[span_16](end_span)[span_17](start_span).
• Rationale: In Canada, parents have the legal right to make health decisions for their children unless the refusal poses an immediate threat of death or serious permanent harm (e.g., refusing a life-saving transfusion)[span_17](end_span)[span_18](start_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Routine vaccination is not considered an 'emergency' that justifies overriding parental consent or involving Child Protection Services[span_18](end_span).

quiz.q: Can parents legally refuse routine childhood vaccinations in Canada?
quiz.a: Yes, their decision must be respected unless there is an immediate life-threatening risk.

title: 🤝 THE HUSBAND IN THE ROOM
content:
A 20-year-old woman presents with stable epigastric pain. [span_19](start_span)Her husband answers all questions and stays by her side constantly[span_19](end_span)[span_20](start_span).

• Next Best Step: Interview the patient alone (without her husband present)[span_20](end_span)[span_21](start_span).
• Logic: Over-involved partners or constant presence during an interview are 'Red Flags' for Intimate Partner Violence (IPV) or coercion[span_21](end_span)[span_22](start_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: You cannot accurately screen for abuse or provide a safe space for disclosure while the potential abuser is in the room[span_22](end_span).

quiz.q: What is the appropriate action when a partner dominates the medical interview and won't leave the patient's side?
quiz.a: Request to interview the patient alone.

title: 🧠 CLANGING (SPEECH PATTERN)
content:
An agitated patient says: 'I did a lot, a little lot, you little lop, lollipop!'[span_23](end_span)[span_24](end_span)[span_25](start_span).

• Definition: Clanging (Clang Association)[span_25](end_span)[span_26](start_span).
• Feature: Speech where sounds/rhymes govern word choice rather than logical meaning[span_26](end_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Often seen in Mania. [span_27](start_span)Differentiate from Neologisms (inventing new words) or Word Salad (random words with no logical connection)[span_27](end_span).

quiz.q: What term describes speech based on rhyming or punning sounds rather than meaning?

title: 🕊️ STOPPING ENTERAL FEEDING
content:
A competent 85-year-old woman with prior throat cancer wants to stop all enteral supplements and fluids[span_28](end_span)[span_29](end_span)[span_30](start_span).

• Next Best Step: Respect the patient's wishes and arrange for palliative support[span_30](end_span)[span_31](start_span).
• Rule: A mentally competent patient has the right to withdraw life-sustaining treatment, including hydration and nutrition[span_31](end_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: This is NOT considered suicide or MAID; it is the refusal of treatment. [span_32](start_span)You do not need an ethics board if the patient is clearly competent[span_32](end_span).

quiz.q: Is a competent patient allowed to refuse tube feeding even if it leads to death?
quiz.a: Yes, the right to refuse treatment is absolute for competent adults.

title: 🫀 INFERIOR MI: BRADYCARDIA
content:
A 55-year-old with acute inferior MI has BP 100/60 and HR 50/min. [span_33](start_span)[span_34](start_span)He is fully alert and pain-free[span_33](end_span)[span_34](end_span)[span_35](start_span).

• Next Best Step: Monitored Observation[span_35](end_span).
• Rationale: Sinus bradycardia is common in inferior MI (vagal tone). [span_36](start_span)If the patient is Asymptomatic and hemodynamically stable, no intervention (like Atropine or Pacing) is needed[span_36](end_span)[span_37](start_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Only treat bradycardia in MI if there are signs of Low Output (confusion, shock, heart failure)[span_37](end_span).

quiz.q: What is the management of asymptomatic sinus bradycardia in a stable patient with an inferior MI?

title: 🎗️ SIMPLE HYPERPLASIA (NO ATYPIA)
content:
A 45-year-old woman (BMI 40) with heavy bleeding has 'Simple Hyperplasia without Atypia' on biopsy[span_38](end_span)[span_39](start_span).

• Best Treatment: Levonorgestrel-IUD (Mirena)[span_39](end_span).
• Logic: Progestins reverse hyperplasia without atypia. [span_40](start_span)The LNG-IUD is highly effective and reduces systemic side effects compared to oral pills[span_40](end_span)[span_41](start_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Hysterectomy is over-treatment for hyperplasia without atypia unless medical management fails or the patient cannot be monitored[span_41](end_span).

quiz.q: What is the first-line treatment for endometrial simple hyperplasia without atypia?

title: 🤰 HTN DRUGS IN PREGNANCY
content:
A 35-year-old woman with essential HTN plans to get pregnant in 6 months[span_42](end_span)[span_43](start_span).

• Safe Option: Nifedipine[span_43](end_span)[span_44](start_span).
• Contraindicated: ACE inhibitors (Captopril) and ARBs (Losartan) are Teratogenic (renal agenesis)[span_44](end_span)[span_45](start_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Methyldopa, Labetalol, and Nifedipine (XL) are the classic safe 'Big 3' for pregnancy in Canada[span_45](end_span).

quiz.q: Which class of antihypertensives is strictly contraindicated during pregnancy?

title: ⚖️ PHARMA HONORARIUMS
content:
A pharma company offers to pay for your travel and an honorarium to speak at a conference[span_46](end_span)[span_47](start_span).

• Best Response: Accept the offer but Disclose the compensation during the presentation[span_47](end_span)[span_48](start_span).
• CMA Rule: Physicians may accept reasonable honorariums for actual work (preparing/giving a talk), but full transparency/disclosure is mandatory to manage the conflict of interest[span_48](end_span)[span_49](start_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: You must use Generic Names and ensure the content is scientifically balanced, not a sales pitch[span_49](end_span).

quiz.q: Is it ethical to accept a speaking fee from a pharmaceutical company?
quiz.a: Yes, provided it is for actual work and the relationship is fully disclosed.

title: 🏳️‍⚧️ PRONOUNS & GENDER IDENTITY
content:
A patient's health card says 'Male', but they are wearing feminine clothing and makeup[span_50](end_span)[span_51](start_span).

• Best Approach: Use the patient's Preferred Name and Gender[span_51](end_span)[span_52](start_span).
• Principle: Respectful communication and affirming a patient's identity are core to the Communicator role in Canada[span_52](end_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT rely on the legal name on the health card if the patient identifies differently. [span_53](start_span)Always ask how they would like to be addressed[span_53](end_span).

quiz.q: How should a physician address a patient whose appearance doesn't match the sex on their health card?
quiz.a: By using the patient's preferred name and gender.

title: 🤰 PARVOVIRUS B19 IN PREGNANCY
content:
A pregnant woman (22 weeks) is exposed to children with 'Fifth Disease' (Slapped-cheek rash)[span_54](end_span)[span_55](start_span).

• Next Best Step: Order Parvovirus IgG and IgM[span_55](end_span)[span_56](start_span).
• Goal: Determine if the mother is immune (IgG+) or has a new infection (IgM+), which risks Fetal Hydrops[span_56](end_span)[span_57](start_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If IgM is positive, the next step is Serial Ultrasounds to check for signs of hydrops (fetal anemia/ascites)[span_57](end_span).

quiz.q: What is the initial investigation for a pregnant woman exposed to Parvovirus B19?

title: 📊 PREVALENCE VS INCIDENCE
content:
You want to estimate the total number of cancer patients currently in your community[span_58](end_span)[span_59](start_span).

• Statistic: Prevalence[span_59](end_span).
• Definition: Total number of existing cases in a population at a specific time. (Incidence = New cases) [span_60](start_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Prevalence is useful for resource planning (knowing how many people need care NOW)[span_60](end_span).

quiz.q: Which statistic measures the total number of people living with a disease at a given time?

title: 🦴 MYOFASCIAL PAIN SYNDROME
content:
A woman has a 2-month history of neck pain, normal neuro exam, but focal 'tender points' that trigger pain on palpation[span_61](end_span)[span_62](end_span)[span_63](start_span).

• Likely Diagnosis: Myofascial Pain Syndrome[span_63](end_span)[span_64](start_span).
• Key: Localized trigger points in muscles with normal reflexes/sensation[span_64](end_span)[span_65](start_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Differentiate from Fibromyalgia (which has widespread pain/11+ points) and PMR (which has high ESR and stiffness in older patients)[span_65](end_span).

quiz.q: What diagnosis is suggested by focal muscle tenderness and 'trigger points' with a normal neurologic exam?

title: 🦴 PSORIATIC ARTHRITIS (SIGNS)
content:
Asymmetrical polyarthritis with Dactylitis ('Sausage digits') and Nail Pitting[span_66](end_span)[span_67](start_span).

• Likely Diagnosis: Psoriatic Arthritis[span_67](end_span).
• Classic Clues: Pitting, 'Oil spots' on nails, and dactylitis. [span_68](start_span)[span_69](start_span)No rash is needed for the exam diagnosis[span_68](end_span)[span_69](end_span)!.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Strongly associated with DIP joint involvement, unlike Rheumatoid Arthritis which spares the DIPs.

quiz.q: What is the term for the 'sausage-like' swelling of digits seen in psoriatic arthritis?

title: 🌬️ LOCULATED EFFUSION (NBS)
content:
A 24-year-old with pneumonia has a loculated (trapped) pleural effusion[span_70](end_span)[span_71](start_span).

• Next Best Step: Diagnostic Thoracentesis[span_71](end_span)[span_72](start_span).
• Logic: You must analyze the fluid (pH, glucose, LDH, gram stain) to distinguish a simple effusion from an Empyema which requires a chest tube[span_72](end_span)[span_73](start_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT just give IV antibiotics and wait; if it's an empyema, the antibiotics won't penetrate well enough without drainage[span_73](end_span).

quiz.q: What is the mandatory next step for a new parapneumonic pleural effusion?

title: 🦷 DENTAL X-RAYS IN PREGNANCY
content:
A woman who had a full set of dental radiographs just found out she is 11 weeks pregnant[span_74](end_span)[span_75](start_span).

• Next Best Step: Reassure the patient of minimal risk[span_75](end_span)[span_76](start_span).
• Fact: The radiation dose from dental X-rays (especially with a lead apron) is negligible and far below the threshold for fetal harm (50 mGy)[span_76](end_span)[span_77](start_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT offer termination or urgent ultrasounds for diagnostic imaging done above the level of the pelvis[span_77](end_span).

quiz.q: Is there a significant risk to the fetus from maternal dental radiographs?
quiz.a: No, the risk is minimal and reassurance is appropriate.

title: 🧠 CLOZAPINE & THE HEART
content:
A patient with treatment-resistant schizophrenia develops chest pain and SOB shortly after starting Clozapine[span_78](end_span)[span_79](start_span).

• High-Risk Side Effect: Myocarditis or Cardiomyopathy[span_79](end_span).
• Action: Check cardiac enzymes and ECG immediately; [span_80](start_span)Clozapine must be stopped if myocarditis is confirmed[span_80](end_span)[span_81](start_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: While Clozapine is famous for Agranulocytosis (WBC monitoring), Myocarditis is a life-threatening cardiovascular risk, especially in the first month[span_81](end_span).

quiz.q: Besides agranulocytosis, what is a major life-threatening risk of Clozapine in the first weeks of treatment?

title: 🩸 MENORRHAGIA (INITIAL STEP)
content:
A 36-year-old smoker with regular but heavy flow. [span_82](start_span)[span_83](start_span)Uterus is normal; biopsy shows secretory endometrium (ovulatory)[span_82](end_span)[span_83](end_span)[span_84](start_span).

• Initial Management: NSAIDs during menstruation[span_84](end_span).
• Logic: NSAIDs reduce prostaglandin levels and can decrease flow by 30%. [span_85](start_span)Since she smokes, combined OCPs are relatively contraindicated if she were >35 (risk of VTE)[span_85](end_span)[span_86](start_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Tranexamic Acid is also a great non-hormonal option if NSAIDs fail[span_86](end_span).

quiz.q: What is a first-line non-hormonal medical treatment for heavy menstrual bleeding?

title: 🫀 ATHLETE SCREENING
content:
A young athlete wants screening after a peer died suddenly on the field[span_87](end_span)[span_88](start_span).

• Next Best Step: Electrocardiography (ECG)[span_88](end_span)[span_89](start_span).
• Goal: Screen for HCM, WPW, or Long QT syndrome which can cause sudden cardiac death in the young[span_89](end_span)[span_90](start_span).

⚠️ Exam Trap: History and Physical are the absolute first step, but the ECG is the standard initial test for screening athletes[span_90](end_span).

quiz.q: What is the most appropriate initial screening test for a young athlete concerned about sudden cardiac death?

title: 👶 BRONCHIOLITIS: MANAGEMENT
content:
A 5-month-old presents with runny nose, cough, wheezing, and SpO2 92%. She has accessory muscle use and crackles.

• Next Best Step: Nasal Suctioning and Supportive Care (Oxygen/Fluids).
• Note: Bronchiolitis is usually viral (RSV). Current Canadian (CPS) guidelines do NOT recommend Ventolin, Epinephrine, or Steroids routinely.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT order a Chest X-ray or start antibiotics unless you suspect a secondary bacterial pneumonia. The treatment is purely supportive.

quiz.q: What is the most appropriate initial treatment for a stable infant with bronchiolitis?

title: 🧠 FACTITIOUS DISORDER
content:
A 35-year-old man has had multiple endoscopies and surgeries for vague symptoms. Investigations are normal. He is found to be deliberately inducing symptoms but has no external motivation (like money or avoiding work).

• Diagnosis: Factitious Disorder.
• Key: The motivation is the 'Sick Role' (internal), not external gain (unlike Malingering).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Somatic Symptom Disorder, the patient is NOT faking; they genuinely feel the symptoms. In Factitious, they are creating them.

quiz.q: What is the primary motivation for a patient with Factitious Disorder?
quiz.a: To assume the 'sick role' (internal psychological need).

title: 🧱 SOFT TISSUE SARCOMA (MGMT)
content:
A 63-year-old man has a 12 cm firm, well-circumscribed mass in his thigh. Biopsy shows rhabdomyosarcoma.

• Next Best Step: Radiotherapy followed by En Bloc Resection.
• Note: For large, high-grade sarcomas, preoperative radiation helps shrink the tumor to allow for limb-sparing surgery.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Amputation is rarely the first choice now; limb-sparing surgery with radiation is the standard of care.

quiz.q: What is the preferred management for a large soft tissue sarcoma of the extremity?
quiz.a: Radiotherapy followed by en bloc surgical resection.

title: 🚑 PATIENT TRANSFER SAFETY
content:
A patient is being transferred to another facility for a procedure. The patient had a seizure recently.

• Next Best Step: Ensure a Nurse or Physician accompanies the patient during the transfer.
• Principle: Ensuring patient safety during transitions of care is a core responsibility. High-risk patients must have appropriate medical supervision during transport.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT delay the transfer just to notify family if the procedure is urgent, but do NOT transfer an unstable patient without staff escort.

quiz.q: What is the priority when transferring a patient who is at risk of acute complications like seizures?
quiz.a: Ensuring a medical staff member (nurse or doctor) accompanies the patient.

title: 🍭 DIABETIC RETINOPATHY: TYPE 1 VS 2
content:
TypeInitial Eye Screen
Type 1 Diabetes5 years after diagnosis
Type 2 DiabetesAt the time of diagnosis

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Type 2 DM, the patient may have had high sugar for years before diagnosis. In Type 1, the onset is sudden, so complications take time to develop.

quiz.q: When should a Type 2 Diabetic patient have their first ophthalmology screening?
quiz.a: Immediately at the time of diagnosis.

title: 🧠 POST-OP DELIRIUM: PROGNOSIS
content:
An 85-year-old becomes confused 2 days after hip surgery. The family asks about the long-term outlook.

• Communication: State that it is common and usually resolves over days to weeks as the underlying cause (pain, meds, infection) is treated.
• Fact: Delirium is a marker of increased mortality and future cognitive decline.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Always perform a 'Medication Review' first in any case of post-op delirium.

quiz.q: What is the typical timeframe for delirium resolution once the cause is addressed?

title: 🌬️ PEDIATRIC ASTHMA (STEP-LADDER)
content:
A 7-year-old is using SABA 4 times a week and waking up twice a month with cough.

• Next Best Step: Add a Daily Low-dose ICS (Inhaled Corticosteroid).
• Logic: If a child needs SABA > 2 times/week, their asthma is uncontrolled and they need a 'preventer' (ICS).

⚠️ Exam Trap: For children < 12y, always start with low-dose ICS before adding a LABA.

quiz.q: What is the first-line maintenance therapy for a child with uncontrolled asthma?

title: 👵 ELDER ABUSE (FINANCIAL)
content:
An elderly patient's son is managing her bank accounts. The patient is well-groomed but mentions she 'cannot afford' her medications despite having a good pension.

• Next Best Step: Interview the patient alone and ask specific questions about her finances and safety.
• Principle: Financial exploitation is a common form of elder abuse. You must ensure the patient's autonomy and safety.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You do not report immediately without more evidence or if a capable patient refuses, but you must investigate the suspicion.

quiz.q: What is the first step when you suspect an elderly patient is being financially exploited by a family member?
quiz.a: Interview the patient alone to assess their safety and finances.

title: ⚖️ CAPACITY IN DEMENTIA
content:
A patient with mild dementia wants to choose his own nursing home. His daughter (SDM) disagrees and wants him elsewhere.

• Decision: Respect the patient's choice if he is capable of this specific task.
• Rule: Capacity is Task-Specific. A patient can have dementia but still be capable of making a simple personal choice like residence.

⚠️ Exam Trap: An SDM only makes decisions if the patient is found incapable for that specific decision.

quiz.q: When does a Substitute Decision Maker (SDM) have the legal authority to make a choice for a patient?
quiz.a: Only when the patient is determined to be mentally incapable of making that specific decision.

title: 🤰 IRON DEFICIENCY IN PREGNANCY
content:
A woman at 28 weeks has a Hemoglobin of $105~g/L$ (Low) and Ferritin of $12~\mu g/L$ (Low).

• Management: Oral Iron Supplementation (Ferrous Sulfate).
• Note: Iron requirements increase significantly in the 2nd and 3rd trimesters due to fetal growth and plasma expansion.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT confuse with 'Physiological Anemia' where Ferritin would be normal. If Ferritin is low, it is Iron Deficiency.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of anemia in the third trimester of pregnancy?

title: 🧪 HYPERLIPIDEMIA (SCREENING)
content:
Canadian Cardiovascular Society (CCS) guidelines.

• Who: Men and Women ≥ 40 years old.
• Test: Fasting or non-fasting Lipid Profile + Framingham Risk Score (FRS).
• Statin Indication: FRS ≥ 20% (High risk) or FRS 10-19% with LDL > 3.5.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Patients with Diabetes, CKD, or AAA are considered high risk automatically and should be on a statin regardless of the score if age ≥ 40.

quiz.q: At what age should routine screening for dyslipidemia begin for average-risk adults in Canada?

title: 🚑 ANAPHYLAXIS: THE PRIORITY
content:
A patient has hives, wheezing, and a BP of 80/40 after a bee sting.

• Next Best Step: IM Epinephrine (mid-outer thigh).
• Note: Epinephrine is the only life-saving medication. Antihistamines and Steroids are secondary and take too long to work.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Never waste time on IV access before giving the first dose of IM Epinephrine if the patient is unstable.

quiz.q: What is the first-line medication for acute anaphylactic shock?

title: 🦴 SCOLIOSIS SCREENING
content:
A 12-year-old girl is found to have a rib hump on the Adam's Forward Bend Test.

• Next Best Step: X-ray of the Spine (PA and Lateral) to measure the Cobb Angle.
• Mgmt: < 20° (Observe); 20-40° (Bracing); > 40-50° (Surgery).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Scoliosis is most common in girls during the adolescent growth spurt. Always check for skin markers (like cafe-au-lait spots) to rule out NF1.

quiz.q: What is the gold standard for measuring the severity of scoliosis?

title: 🤝 INTIMATE PARTNER VIOLENCE (IPV)
content:
A woman has injuries that don't match her story. You suspect IPV.

• Next Best Step: Assess Immediate Safety and provide a list of local resources/shelters.
• Principle: You cannot report abuse of a capable adult to the police without their consent. Your role is to support and offer safety planning.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Mandatory reporting only applies to Children and (in some provinces) seniors in long-term care.

quiz.q: What is the physician's primary responsibility when a capable adult discloses domestic violence?
quiz.a: Safety assessment and providing resources (not mandatory reporting to police).

title: 🗣️ BREAKING BAD NEWS (SPIKES)
content:
A patient is diagnosed with terminal pancreatic cancer.

• Action: Use the SPIKES protocol. Start by asking what the patient knows (Perception).
• Key: "What is your understanding of your illness so far?".

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT start with the facts (Knowledge) before assessing what the patient already knows and wants to know.

quiz.q: What is the first step of the SPIKES protocol for breaking bad news?
quiz.a: Setting (Privacy) followed by Perception (assessing the patient's understanding).

title: 🔪 POST-OP WOUND INFECTION
content:
A patient has a red, warm, and painful incision 5 days after abdominal surgery.

• Next Best Step: Open and Drain the wound (Incision and Drainage).
• Logic: For a surgical site infection (SSI), drainage is more important than antibiotics alone.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has Systemic symptoms (high fever, tachycardia), then start IV antibiotics in addition to drainage.

quiz.q: What is the primary management for a localized surgical site infection?
quiz.a: Incision and drainage of the wound.

title: 🤰 GBS PROPHYLAXIS (TIMING)
content:
A woman is GBS positive on her 36-week swab.

• Next Best Step: IV Penicillin G at the onset of labor or rupture of membranes.
• Note: You do NOT treat the GBS carrier state before labor as it will just recur.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has a Penicillin allergy (not anaphylaxis), use Cefazolin. If severe allergy, use Clindamycin (if sensitive).

quiz.q: When is the most effective time to administer antibiotics for GBS prophylaxis?

title: 🌬️ COPD: STEP-WISE MGMT
content:
SeverityTreatment (CTS Guidelines)
MildPRN SABA or SAMA
ModerateLAMA or LABA
Severe / ExacerbationsLAMA + LABA + ICS

⚠️ Exam Trap: In COPD, LAMA (e.g., Tiotropium) is generally more effective at preventing exacerbations than LABA. Smoking Cessation is the only intervention that slows the decline in FEV1.

quiz.q: What is the single most effective intervention for reducing the progression of COPD?

title: 🧠 DEPRESSION IN ADOLESCENTS
content:
A 16-year-old girl has low mood, insomnia, and poor grades for 3 weeks.

• First-line Treatment: Fluoxetine (Prozac) + CBT/IPT.
• Note: Fluoxetine is the only SSRI with strong evidence for efficacy and safety in children and adolescents.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Monitor closely for Suicidal Ideation in the first 2-4 weeks of starting any SSRI in a young person.

quiz.q: What is the first-line SSRI for treating depression in adolescents?

title: 🏆 CANADIAN SCREENING: TOP 5
content:
1. Cervical Cancer: Pap every 3 years (age 25-69).
2. Breast Cancer: Mammogram every 2 years (age 50-74).
3. Colon Cancer: FIT every 2 years (age 50-74).
4. AAA: One-time Ultrasound (Men age 65-80).
5. Lung Cancer: Annual LDCT (High-risk, age 55-74).

🌟 CONGRATULATIONS DOCTOR! 🌟
You have hit 700 cards. You have covered the core of the MCC files. This is your arsenal. Go conquer!

quiz.q: What is the routine screening test for colorectal cancer in average-risk individuals in Canada?

title: 🎗️ MALE INFERTILITY: FIRST STEP
content:
A couple has been trying to conceive for 14 months without success. Both are 28 years old.

• Next Best Step: Semen Analysis.
• Logic: It is the least invasive, least expensive, and quickest test to rule out male factors, which contribute to 40-50% of infertility cases.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT start invasive testing for the female (like HSG) until the semen analysis is confirmed normal.

quiz.q: What is the initial investigation for a couple presenting with infertility?

title: 🤰 MOLAR PREGNANCY: FOLLOW-UP
content:
A patient undergoes suction and curettage for a hydatidiform mole.

• Mandatory Follow-up: Serial β-hCG levels weekly until zero, then monthly for 6 months.
• Crucial Advice: Reliable Contraception (OCPs preferred) during the follow-up period.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient gets pregnant during follow-up, you cannot distinguish a new pregnancy from Choriocarcinoma. If hCG levels rise or plateau, suspect malignancy.

quiz.q: How long must a patient use contraception after a molar pregnancy evacuation?

title: 🍺 ALCOHOL USE: BRIEF INTERVENTION
content:
A patient drinks 4-5 beers daily but has no withdrawal symptoms or social impairment yet.

• Next Best Step: Brief Intervention (Feedback, Advice, and Goal Setting).
• Logic: For 'at-risk' drinkers, a brief counseling session by a physician significantly reduces long-term alcohol consumption.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do not refer to rehab or start Naltrexone unless the patient meets the full DSM-5 criteria for Alcohol Use Disorder. Start with the least intensive effective intervention.

quiz.q: What is the most appropriate initial management for at-risk drinking?

title: 🦵 ACUTE LIMB ISCHEMIA (6 P's)
content:
Surgical Emergency! Sudden onset of leg pain and coldness in a patient with AFib.

The 6 P's: Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Poikilothermia (Coldness).
• Next Best Step: IV Heparin Bolus + Urgent Surgical Consult.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If the leg is paralyzed or has no sensation (late signs), the limb may be unsalvageable. Do NOT delay heparin for imaging if the diagnosis is clinically certain.

quiz.q: What is the first medical intervention for suspected acute limb ischemia?

title: 👶 KAWASAKI DISEASE (COMPLICATIONS)
content:
A 3-year-old with fever for 6 days, conjunctivitis, and a strawberry tongue.

• Priority Investigation: Echocardiogram (at diagnosis, 2 weeks, and 6 weeks).
• Main Concern: Coronary Artery Aneurysms (occurs in 25% of untreated cases).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Treatment is IVIG + High-dose Aspirin. This is the only pediatric scenario where high-dose aspirin is used despite the risk of Reye's Syndrome.

quiz.q: What is the most serious complication of Kawasaki disease?

title: ⚖️ BOUNDARIES: THE SMALL GIFT
content:
A patient brings you a box of chocolates or a hand-knit scarf to thank you for their care.

• Action: Accept the gift graciously.
• Rule: Small, token, or culturally appropriate gifts that do not influence care are acceptable. Rejecting them can damage the therapeutic relationship.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Always decline Cash or Expensive gifts (e.g., jewelry, cars) as they represent a boundary violation and potential conflict of interest.

quiz.q: Can a physician accept a small token gift from a patient?
quiz.a: Yes, if it is of nominal value and does not compromise the professional relationship.

title: 🧪 NEPHROTIC SYNDROME: INITIAL TX
content:
A child presents with periorbital edema and 4+ proteinuria. Diagnosis: Minimal Change Disease (MCD).

• Next Best Step: Corticosteroids (Prednisone).
• Logic: 90% of children with nephrotic syndrome have MCD and respond rapidly to steroids. Renal biopsy is not needed initially in children.


⚠️ Exam Trap: These patients are at high risk for Infection (S. pneumoniae) and Thrombosis (loss of antithrombin III). Ensure their immunizations are up to date.

quiz.q: What is the first-line treatment for minimal change disease in children?

title: 🤰 VTE PROPHYLAXIS IN PREGNANCY
content:
A pregnant woman has a history of a previous DVT while on OCPs.

• Management: Prophylactic LMWH (Enoxaparin) throughout pregnancy and 6 weeks postpartum.
• Note: Warfarin is contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Pregnancy is a hypercoagulable state. For high-risk patients, LMWH is the gold standard as it does not cross the placenta.

quiz.q: Which anticoagulant is preferred for prophylaxis and treatment during pregnancy?

title: 👶 UTI IMAGING (RBUS VS VCUG)
content:
A 1-year-old boy has his first febrile UTI.

• Step 1: Renal and Bladder Ultrasound (RBUS).
• Step 2: VCUG only if the RBUS shows hydronephrosis/scarring or if the UTI is recurrent.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT order a VCUG for a first-time simple UTI in a child with a normal ultrasound. The goal is to avoid unnecessary radiation.

quiz.q: What is the initial imaging study for a child under 2 years with their first febrile UTI?

title: 🧱 ACUTE MESENTERIC ISCHEMIA
content:
Surgical Emergency! An elderly patient with AFib presents with 'Pain out of proportion' to the physical exam.

• Next Best Step: CT Angiography (of the abdomen).
• Management: Immediate surgery (Embolectomy/Bowel resection) and IV Heparin.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If you wait for signs of peritonitis (rebound/guarding), the bowel is already dead. High lactate is a late and poor prognostic sign.

quiz.q: What is the classic clinical phrase for the presentation of acute mesenteric ischemia?
quiz.a: Pain out of proportion to physical examination findings.

title: 🧠 ACUTE MANIA: TREATMENT
content:
A patient presents with pressured speech, grandiosity, and decreased need for sleep for 1 week.

• First-line (Acute): Lithium, Valproate, or an Atypical Antipsychotic (e.g., Quetiapine, Risperidone).
• Note: If the patient is agitated/aggressive, an antipsychotic often works faster.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT use SSRIs (antidepressants) in a patient with suspected bipolar disorder as it can trigger a manic switch.

quiz.q: What is the first-line medication class for acute management of a manic episode?

title: 📊 SENSITIVITY VS SPECIFICITY (RECALL)
content:
MeasureGoal
SensitivityRule OUT (Screening). Few false negatives.
SpecificityRule IN (Confirmation). Few false positives.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Sensitivity is the ability of a test to correctly identify those with the disease. Specificity is identifying those without the disease.

quiz.q: Which statistical measure is most important for a screening test?

title: ⚖️ JEHOVAH'S WITNESS MINOR (EMERGENCY)
content:
A 10-year-old child of Jehovah's Witness parents is bleeding out after an accident. The parents refuse transfusion.

• Next Best Step: Transfuse the child immediately.
• Rule: In an emergency, the physician's duty to save a child's life overrides parental refusal. You do not need a court order in a life-or-death emergency.

⚠️ Exam Trap: This applies only to minors. For a competent adult Jehovah's Witness, you must respect their refusal even if it leads to death.

quiz.q: Can a physician provide a life-saving blood transfusion to a minor against parental religious objection in an emergency?

title: 🫀 HEART FAILURE: NYHA CLASS
content:
ClassDescription
INo symptoms with ordinary activity.
IISymptoms with Ordinary activity (e.g., walking 2 blocks).
IIISymptoms with Less than ordinary activity.
IVSymptoms at Rest.

⚠️ Exam Trap: NYHA class measures functional capacity and can change. ACC/AHA Stages (A-D) measure structural disease and only progress forward.

quiz.q: A patient who is comfortable at rest but has shortness of breath when dressing themselves is in which NYHA class?

title: 🎗️ ENDOMETRIAL CANCER RISK FACTORS
content:
Associated with Unopposed Estrogen.

• Risk Factors: Obesity (#1), PCOS, Nulliparity, Early menarche/Late menopause, Tamoxifen use.
• Protective: OCPs, Smoking (paradoxically decreases estrogen).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Obesity is the most significant risk factor in the West due to the peripheral conversion of androgens to estrone in adipose tissue.

quiz.q: What is the primary risk factor for endometrial cancer in a postmenopausal woman?

title: 👶 FINE MOTOR MILESTONES
content:
• 6 Months: Transfers objects hand-to-hand.
• 9 Months: Pincer grasp (immature).
• 12 Months: Mature pincer grasp (thumb and forefinger).
• 2 Years: Tower of 6 blocks.
• 3 Years: Copies a Circle.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Hand preference (handedness) should not appear before 18-24 months. If a 6-month-old only uses their right hand, investigate for a neurological deficit (e.g., CP).

quiz.q: At what age should a child be able to copy a circle?

title: 🔪 POST-OP FEVER (THE 5 W'S)
content:
TimingCauseAction
Day 1-2Wind (Atelectasis/Pneumonia)Incentive spirometry
Day 3Water (UTI)UA/Culture
Day 5Walking (DVT/PE)LMWH/Ultrasound
Day 7Wound (Infection)Drainage/Antibiotics
Any DayWonder drugsStop medication

⚠️ Exam Trap: A fever within the first 24 hours post-op is often physiological (stress response) and doesn't require antibiotics unless very high or associated with symptoms.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of fever on post-operative day 3?

title: 🌬️ ASTHMA: CANADIAN CTS GUIDELINES
content:
Diagnosis: Spirometry showing reversible obstruction (increase in FEV1 > 12%).

• Step 1 (Mild): PRN SABA.
• Step 2 (Moderate): Add Low-dose ICS (Inhaled Corticosteroid).
• Step 3: Add LABA (Combination inhaler).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Canadian guidelines now emphasize that for patients requiring SABA > 2 times/week, an ICS is mandatory to reduce airway inflammation and prevent death.

quiz.q: What is the gold standard for diagnosing asthma in a 10-year-old?

title: 🚗 FITNESS TO DRIVE: VISUAL FIELD
content:
A patient has a stroke and develops Homonymous Hemianopsia (loss of half the visual field in both eyes).

• Action: Inform the patient they must not drive and report to the Ministry of Transportation (MOT).
• Rule: Visual field requirements for driving in Canada generally require 120 continuous degrees along the horizontal meridian.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In most provinces, reporting is mandatory for physicians. Always check your provincial guidelines, but for the MCCQE1, reporting 'unfit to drive' is the standard.

quiz.q: Is a physician legally required to report a patient who is medically unfit to drive in Canada?

title: 💎 THE DIAMOND SUMMARY: 701-720
content:
1. Infertility: Semen analysis first.
2. Nephrotic (Peds): Steroids first.
3. Meningitis: Antibiotics + Steroids first.
4. AAA (Unstable): OR first (no CT).
5. Trauma: ABCs always first.
6. Anaphylaxis: IM Epinephrine first.
7. Cauda Equina: MRI first.

🌟 YOU HAVE REACHED CARD 720! 🌟
You are building an unstoppable knowledge base. Keep going, Canada's future physician!

quiz.q: What is the next best step for an unstable patient with suspected ruptured AAA?

title: 🧠 ACUTE MIGRAINE: MANAGEMENT
content:
A 30-year-old presents with a severe unilateral, throbbing headache with photophobia.

• First-line (Mild/Mod): NSAIDs (Naproxen) or Acetaminophen.
• First-line (Severe): Triptans (e.g., Sumatriptan).
• Next Best Step: Combine a Triptan with an NSAID for better efficacy.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Contraindications to Triptans: Ischemic heart disease, Prinzmetal angina, or uncontrolled HTN (due to vasoconstriction). Use Metoclopramide as an adjunct for nausea.

quiz.q: Which class of medications is the first-line choice for a severe acute migraine attack?

title: 👶 MECKEL’S DIVERTICULUM
content:
A 2-year-old presents with sudden, painless massive rectal bleeding (maroon/brick-red stools).

• Next Best Step: Meckel Scan (Technetium-99m pertechnetate scan).
• Logic: The scan identifies ectopic gastric mucosa which causes the ulceration and bleeding.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Meckel's follows the Rule of 2s: 2% of population, 2 feet from ileocecal valve, 2 inches long, 2 types of ectopic tissue, and presents by age 2.

quiz.q: What is the diagnostic test of choice for Meckel’s diverticulum?

title: ⚖️ REPORTING GUNSHOT WOUNDS
content:
A patient arrives at the ED with a stable gunshot wound to the leg.

• Action: Mandatory Reporting to Police.
• Principle: In all Canadian provinces, physicians are legally required to report gunshot wounds to the local police department immediately.

⚠️ Exam Trap: This reporting requirement overrides patient confidentiality. However, for Stab Wounds, reporting is generally only mandatory if it poses an ongoing risk to public safety or the patient.

quiz.q: Is a physician legally required to report a gunshot wound to the police in Canada?
quiz.a: Yes, it is a mandatory reporting requirement.

title: 🦵 CHRONIC VENOUS INSUFFICIENCY
content:
A 55-year-old with leg heaviness, pitting edema, and Hyperpigmentation (hemosiderin staining) around the ankles.

• Initial Management: Leg Elevation and Compression Stockings.
• Complication: Venous Stasis Ulcers (usually over the medial malleolus).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Differentiate from PAD: Venous issues have Normal Pulses and edema; Arterial issues have Absent Pulses and thin/shiny skin.

quiz.q: What is the mainstay of conservative management for chronic venous insufficiency?

title: 🩸 FEBRILE NEUTROPENIA (MGMT)
content:
Medical Emergency! A cancer patient on chemo has a temp of $38.5^{\circ}C$ and ANC < 0.5.

• Next Best Step: Empiric IV Antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam) immediately after blood cultures.
• Logic: You must cover Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Do NOT wait for culture results or X-rays.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In febrile neutropenia, the patient may not have typical signs of infection (like pus or CXR infiltrates) because they don't have enough WBCs to create an inflammatory response.

quiz.q: What is the priority in a patient with febrile neutropenia?
quiz.a: Immediate administration of broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (covering Pseudomonas).

title: 🦠 OSTEOMYELITIS (DIAGNOSIS)
content:
A patient with a deep foot ulcer (Diabetes) has localized bone pain and fever.

• Best Initial Test: X-ray (may be normal for 2 weeks).
• Most Sensitive Imaging: MRI.
• Gold Standard: Bone Biopsy and Culture.

⚠️ Exam Trap: 'Probe to Bone' test: If you can touch bone with a sterile metal probe through a diabetic ulcer, the probability of osteomyelitis is > 90%.

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive imaging modality for early diagnosis of osteomyelitis?

title: 👶 BREAST MILK VS BREASTFEEDING JAUNDICE
content:
FeatureBreastfeeding (Failure) JaundiceBreast Milk Jaundice
TimingDays 2 - 4Days 4 - 7 (Peaks at 2 weeks)
CauseInadequate intake / DehydrationSubstance in milk (glucuronidase)
SignsWeight loss, few wet diapersHealthy baby, growing well

⚠️ Exam Trap: Management for Breastfeeding Jaundice is to increase frequency of feeding. For Breast Milk Jaundice, continue breastfeeding (reassurance).

quiz.q: Which type of neonatal jaundice is caused by inadequate milk intake and dehydration?

title: 🔪 CECAL VS SIGMOID VOLVULUS
content:
FeatureSigmoid VolvulusCecal Volvulus
PatientElderly, bedriddenYounger adults
X-ray'Coffee Bean' (RUQ to LLQ)'Embryo' / 'Teardrop' (LLQ to RUQ)
Initial TxSigmoidoscopic DetorsionEmergency Surgery

⚠️ Exam Trap: Cecal volvulus often requires surgery (ileocolectomy) immediately because endoscopic detorsion is rarely successful and the risk of ischemia is higher.

quiz.q: Which type of colonic volvulus can often be managed initially with sigmoidoscopic decompression?

title: 🦋 OSTEOPOROSIS: DRUG HOLIDAY
content:
A 70-year-old woman has been on Alendronate for 5 years. Her BMD has stabilized.

• Next Best Step: Consider a 'Drug Holiday' (Stopping for 2-3 years).
