• Reason: To reduce the risk of rare long-term side effects: Osteonecrosis of the Jaw (ONJ) and Atypical Femoral Fractures.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A drug holiday is usually only considered after 5 years of oral or 3 years of IV bisphosphonates in patients at low-to-moderate fracture risk.

quiz.q: What are two serious long-term complications of prolonged bisphosphonate use?
quiz.a: Osteonecrosis of the jaw and atypical femoral fractures.

title: 🧠 CONFUSION ASSESSMENT METHOD (CAM)
content:
Used to diagnose Delirium at the bedside.

The Criteria (Need 1, 2, and either 3 or 4):
1. Acute onset and fluctuating course.
2. Inattention.
3. Disorganized thinking.
4. Altered level of consciousness.

⚠️ Exam Trap: CAM is highly sensitive and specific. If a patient meets these criteria, the next best step is to find the Medical Cause (UTI, Drugs, Electrolytes).

quiz.q: What bedside tool is the gold standard for identifying delirium?

title: 🤰 BIOPHYSICAL PROFILE (BPP) SCORING
content:
ComponentScore 2 (Normal)Score 0 (Abnormal)
Fetal Breathing1+ episode (30s)None
Fetal Movements3+ body movements< 3
Fetal Tone1+ limb extension/flexionNone
Amniotic Fluid (AFI)Pocket > 2cmNone
NSTReactiveNon-reactive

⚠️ Exam Trap: Total score is out of 10 (or 8 without NST). A score of 0-4 is an indication for Urgent Delivery. A score of 6 requires repeat testing in 24h.

quiz.q: What BPP score range is generally an indication for immediate delivery?

title: 🧴 PITYRIASIS ROSEA
content:
A 20-year-old has a single 4cm scaly pink patch on the back, followed 1 week later by multiple smaller oval lesions in a 'Christmas Tree' distribution.

• Next Best Step: Reassurance and symptomatic treatment (antihistamines for itch).
• Key Feature: The initial large lesion is the 'Herald Patch'.


⚠️ Exam Trap: It is self-limiting and resolves in 6-8 weeks. If the rash involves the palms and soles, you must rule out Secondary Syphilis with an RPR.

quiz.q: What is the name of the initial large lesion seen in Pityriasis Rosea?

title: ⚖️ TREATING FAMILY MEMBERS
content:
Your sister asks you for a prescription for an antidepressant because she is 'feeling down'.

• Best Action: Politely decline and encourage her to see her own family physician.
• CMA/CPSO Rule: Physicians should not treat family members or themselves except for minor or emergency situations where no other physician is available.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Treating family members compromises objectivity and can lead to substandard care. Psychological issues are never considered 'minor' in this context.

quiz.q: When is it acceptable for a physician to treat a family member in Canada?
quiz.a: Only for minor conditions or in emergency situations when no other doctor is available.

title: 🧱 ISCHEMIC COLITIS VS MESENTERIC ISCHEMIA
content:
FeatureAcute Mesenteric IschemiaIschemic Colitis
LocationSmall Bowel (SMA)Colon (Watershed areas)
PainSevere, out of proportionModerate, crampy
BleedingLate/NoneHematochezia (Bloody stools)
Next StepCT Angio / SurgeryColonoscopy

⚠️ Exam Trap: Ischemic colitis often follows a period of Hypotension (e.g., after a major surgery or marathon run). The 'watershed' areas (Splenic flexure) are most vulnerable.

quiz.q: Which condition typically presents with crampy abdominal pain followed by bloody diarrhea in an elderly patient?

title: 🚑 PELVIC FRACTURE (UNSTABLE)
content:
A trauma patient has an unstable 'Open Book' pelvic fracture and is hypotensive.

• Immediate Next Step: Pelvic Binder (at the level of the greater trochanters).
• Rationale: To decrease pelvic volume and tamponade venous bleeding.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT perform 'pelvic rocking' or repeated exams of the pelvis in trauma, as this can dislodge clots and worsen life-threatening bleeding.

quiz.q: What is the priority intervention for a hemodynamically unstable patient with a suspected pelvic fracture?

title: 🧪 NEPHROGENIC DIABETES INSIPIDUS
content:
A patient on Lithium has polyuria and polydipsia. Sodium is high. ADH is high but the kidney doesn't respond.

• Next Best Step: Water deprivation test (osmolarity won't increase) followed by Desmopressin (no response).
• Management: Discontinue Lithium (if possible). Treatment: Hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ) or Amiloride.

⚠️ Exam Trap: It seems paradoxical to give a diuretic (HCTZ) for polyuria, but it works by causing mild volume depletion, which increases proximal tubule sodium/water reabsorption.

quiz.q: Which medication is most commonly associated with causing nephrogenic diabetes insipidus?

title: 🤰 CORD PROLAPSE
content:
During labor, the umbilical cord is felt on vaginal exam or seen at the introitus.

• Immediate Next Step: Elevate the fetal presenting part (push it up manually) and keep your hand there. Do NOT remove your hand!
• Definitive Step: Emergency C-Section.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT try to push the cord back inside. Position the mother in Knee-Chest or Trendelenburg to help gravity relieve pressure on the cord.

quiz.q: What is the immediate manual maneuver when an umbilical cord prolapse is diagnosed?
quiz.a: Manually elevating the fetal presenting part to relieve cord compression.

title: 📊 ODDS RATIO VS RELATIVE RISK
content:
MeasureStudy TypeCalculation
Relative Risk (RR)Cohort[a/(a+b)] / [c/(c+d)]
Odds Ratio (OR)Case-Control(a/c) / (b/d) = ad/bc

⚠️ Exam Trap: RR can only be calculated if you know the 'Incidence' (how many new cases over time). In Case-Control studies, you start with the outcome, so you can only calculate the OR.

quiz.q: In which type of study design is the calculation of Relative Risk (RR) appropriate?

title: 🦠 LYME DISEASE: PROPHYLAXIS
content:
A patient brings in a tick they removed after a hike in a high-risk area (e.g., Kingston, ON).

• Criteria for Prophylaxis:
1. Tick is Ixodes scapularis.
2. Attached for ≥ 36 hours.
3. Prophylaxis started within 72 hours of removal.
• Tx: Single dose of Doxycycline 200mg.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the tick was attached for < 36 hours, the risk of transmission is near zero; no prophylaxis is needed.

quiz.q: What is the antibiotic used for post-exposure prophylaxis of Lyme disease?

title: 🫀 HEART FAILURE: TARGETS
content:
A patient with HFrEF (EF 30%) is already on an ACEi and Beta-blocker. BP is 115/70.

• Next Best Step: Add an MRA (Mineralocorticoid Receptor Antagonist) like Spironolactone.
• Alternative: If still symptomatic, switch ACEi to ARNI (Sacubitril/Valsartan).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Always check Potassium and Creatinine before starting Spironolactone, as it can cause life-threatening hyperkalemia.

quiz.q: Which medication should be added to ACEi and Beta-blocker therapy in HFrEF to further reduce mortality?

title: 👶 NEONATAL JAUNDICE: PHOTOTHERAPY
content:
A 3-day-old full-term infant has a total bilirubin of $250~\mu mol/L$. He is breastfeeding well.

• Next Best Step: Check the Canadian Paediatric Society (CPS) Nomogram to determine the threshold for phototherapy based on age in hours and risk factors.
• Management: Intensive phototherapy is indicated if the level exceeds the threshold.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT stop breastfeeding while the baby is under phototherapy. In fact, increasing the frequency of feeding helps eliminate bilirubin through stools.

quiz.q: What is the primary tool used in Canada to decide on phototherapy for a newborn?
quiz.a: The CPS (Canadian Paediatric Society) bilirubin nomogram.

title: 👁️ ACUTE ANGLE-CLOSURE GLAUCOMA
content:
Ocular Emergency! Sudden eye pain, headache, blurred vision, and seeing 'Halos' around lights. The eye is red and the pupil is mid-dilated and fixed.

• Next Best Step: Immediate Ophthalmology Referral and pressure-lowering drops (e.g., Timolol, Pilocarpine).
• Definitive Tx: Laser Peripheral Iridotomy.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT dilate the pupil (avoid Atropine) as this will worsen the angle closure and can lead to permanent blindness.

quiz.q: What is the definitive surgical treatment for acute angle-closure glaucoma?

title: 👵 URINARY INCONTINENCE: TYPES
content:
TypeMechanismTypical Presentation
StressWeak pelvic floor / SphincterLeaking with Coughing/Sneezing
UrgeDetrusor OveractivitySudden, intense Urgency
OverflowImpaired contraction (Diabetes/BPH)Constant Dribbling

⚠️ Exam Trap: For Stress, the first-line is Pelvic Floor (Kegel) exercises. For Urge, it is bladder training and Antimuscarinics (Oxybutynin).

quiz.q: Which type of incontinence is characterized by leakage during physical exertion or sneezing?

title: 💊 OCP CONTRAINDICATIONS
content:
Combined Oral Contraceptive Pills (OCPs) contain Estrogen and Progesterone.

• Absolute Contraindications:
1. Smokers ≥ 35 years old.
2. History of VTE/Stroke.
3. Migraine with Aura.
4. Breast Cancer.

⚠️ Exam Trap: For women with these contraindications, the safest effective options are Progesterone-only pills (Mini-pill) or a Copper IUD.

quiz.q: Is a history of migraine with aura a contraindication for combined oral contraceptives?
quiz.a: Yes, due to an increased risk of ischemic stroke.

title: 🧱 SMALL BOWEL OBSTRUCTION (SBO)
content:
Colicky pain, vomiting, and abdominal distension. Most common cause: Adhesions (prior surgery).

• Next Best Step: NPO + IV Fluids + NG Tube Suction (Decompression).
• Note: Most cases resolve with conservative management. If there is fever or peritonitis, suspect Strangulation and go to surgery.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the X-ray shows 'Free Air' under the diaphragm, it is a Perforation, not just an obstruction.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of small bowel obstruction in adults?

title: 🤰 MAGNESIUM SULFATE: TOXICITY
content:
Used for seizure prevention in Preeclampsia/Eclampsia.

• Signs of Toxicity (In order):
1. Loss of deep tendon reflexes (DTRs) - First sign.
2. Respiratory depression.
3. Cardiac arrest.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If toxicity is suspected, the immediate antidote is IV Calcium Gluconate. Monitor urine output closely as Magnesium is renally excreted.

quiz.q: What is the first clinical sign of Magnesium Sulfate toxicity?

title: 👶 DDH SCREENING
content:
Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip (DDH).

• Physical Exam: Barlow and Ortolani maneuvers (up to 3 months); Galeazzi sign (after 3 months).
• Imaging: Ultrasound of the hips if < 6 months; X-ray if > 6 months.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Risk factors include Breech presentation, female sex, and family history. In Canada, an ultrasound is often ordered at 6 weeks for infants with high-risk factors even if the exam is normal.

quiz.q: What is the preferred imaging modality for DDH in an infant under 4-6 months of age?

title: 🧠 LITHIUM SIDE EFFECTS
content:
Common effects even at therapeutic levels.

• Early: Fine hand tremor, polyuria/polydipsia, nausea.
• Long-term: Hypothyroidism and Weight Gain.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the tremor becomes Coarse or the patient is confused/ataxic, suspect Lithium Toxicity (> 1.5 mmol/L).

quiz.q: Which endocrine organ is most commonly affected by long-term Lithium therapy?

title: ⚖️ DATING FORMER PATIENTS
content:
A patient you treated 2 years ago for a broken leg asks you out on a date.

• Rule: Sexual relationships with current patients are always prohibited. For former patients, it depends on the nature of the relationship.
• Psychiatry: Never allowed (once a patient, always a patient).
• Other specialties: May be allowed after a significant period, but generally discouraged.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The more vulnerable the patient or the longer the professional relationship, the more unethical a romantic relationship becomes.

quiz.q: Is it ever ethical for a psychiatrist to date a former patient?
quiz.a: No, it is considered a permanent professional boundary violation.

title: 📊 CANADIAN SCREENING: RECAP
content:
CancerTestFrequency / Age
CervicalPap TestEvery 3y (Age 25-69)
ColorectalFITEvery 2y (Age 50-74)
BreastMammogramEvery 2y (Age 50-74)

⚠️ Exam Trap: These are for average-risk individuals. If there is a strong family history (e.g., 1st degree relative with colon cancer at age 40), screening starts 10 years earlier.

quiz.q: At what age does routine cervical cancer screening (Pap test) start in most Canadian provinces?

title: 🔪 APPENDICITIS IN FEMALES
content:
A 22-year-old woman has RLQ pain. Pregnancy test is negative.

• Next Best Step: Ultrasound of the abdomen/pelvis.
• Reason: To rule out gynecological causes (ovarian cyst, torsion, PID) before diagnosing appendicitis.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Males, appendicitis is often a clinical diagnosis. In Females, imaging is almost always required due to the broader differential.

quiz.q: What is the primary reason for performing an ultrasound in a young female with suspected appendicitis?

title: 🧪 SIADH (DIAGNOSIS)
content:
Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone.

• Labs: Low Serum Sodium (< 135), High Urine Sodium (> 20), and High Urine Osmolality (> 100).
• Clinical: Euvolemic (no edema, no dehydration).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Common causes include Small Cell Lung Cancer, Head Trauma, and SSRIs. First-line treatment is Fluid Restriction.

quiz.q: What is the typical volume status of a patient with SIADH?

title: 🎗️ POST-COITAL BLEEDING
content:
A 35-year-old woman reports bleeding after intercourse.

• Absolute Next Step: Speculum Examination and Cervical Visualization.
• Possible Causes: Cervical cancer, Cervical polyps, or Cervicitis (e.g., Chlamydia).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Even if her last Pap test was normal, you MUST visualize the cervix to rule out a visible lesion or malignancy.

quiz.q: What is the most important initial physical examination for post-coital bleeding?

title: 👶 VENTRICULAR SEPTAL DEFECT (VSD)
content:
The most common congenital heart defect.

• Murmur: Holosystolic, harsh murmur heard at the lower left sternal border.
• Note: Smaller VSDs actually have louder murmurs than larger ones.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Most small VSDs close spontaneously. Large VSDs can lead to Heart Failure (poor feeding, failure to thrive) and may require surgical repair.

quiz.q: What is the characteristic murmur of a VSD?
quiz.a: Holosystolic (pansystolic) murmur at the left lower sternal border.

title: 🦴 GIANT CELL ARTERITIS: TX
content:
Sudden vision loss, headache, and jaw claudication in a 70-year-old.

• Immediate Next Step: High-dose Systemic Steroids (Prednisone).
• Action: Start steroids before performing the temporal artery biopsy to prevent permanent blindness in the other eye.

⚠️ Exam Trap: ESR and CRP are almost always highly elevated. If suspicion is high, the biopsy can be delayed for up to 1-2 weeks without affecting accuracy much.

quiz.q: Should treatment for GCA be delayed to wait for biopsy results?
quiz.a: No, start steroids immediately upon clinical suspicion.

title: 🧠 SEROTONIN SYNDROME: SIGNS
content:
Occurs when combining multiple serotonergic drugs (e.g., SSRI + MAOI or St. John's Wort).

• Triad: 1. Altered Mental Status, 2. Autonomic Instability (Fever, Tachycardia), 3. Neuromuscular Hyperactivity (Clonus, Hyperreflexia).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Clonus (rhythmic muscle contraction) is the most specific sign. Contrast with NMS, which has 'Lead-pipe' rigidity and no clonus.

quiz.q: What is the hallmark physical exam finding that distinguishes Serotonin Syndrome from NMS?

title: 🤫 CONFIDENTIALITY: FAMILY MEMBERS
content:
A patient's daughter calls to ask about her father's lab results. He is a mentally capable adult.

• Action: Refuse to disclose the results without the patient's explicit consent.
• Rule: Confidentiality belongs to the patient. Being a family member does not grant an automatic right to medical information.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The only exception is if the patient is Incapable and the daughter is the legal Substitute Decision Maker (SDM).

quiz.q: Can a physician share medical information with a spouse or child without the patient's consent?
quiz.a: No, unless the patient has provided explicit permission or is incapable and they are the SDM.

title: 🌬️ COPD: OXYGEN THERAPY
content:
Long-term Oxygen Therapy (LTOT) is the only treatment besides smoking cessation that improves mortality in COPD.

• Criteria:
1. PaO2 ≤ 55 mmHg (or SpO2 ≤ 88%).
2. PaO2 56-59 mmHg if there is Cor Pulmonale or Polycythemia.

⚠️ Exam Trap: To be effective, the patient must use the oxygen for at least 15 hours per day.

quiz.q: What is the survival benefit of oxygen therapy in severe COPD?

title: 🔪 CHOLECYSTITIS: HIDA SCAN
content:
A patient has RUQ pain and fever. Ultrasound is inconclusive but suspicion of cholecystitis remains high.

• Next Best Step: HIDA Scan (Cholescintigraphy).
• Result: Non-visualization of the gallbladder confirms acute cholecystitis.

⚠️ Exam Trap: HIDA is more sensitive than Ultrasound for detecting cystic duct obstruction, which is the hallmark of acute cholecystitis.

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive diagnostic imaging study for acute cholecystitis?

title: 🩸 B12 VS FOLATE DEFICIENCY
content:
Both cause macrocytic anemia.

• B12 Deficiency: Neurological symptoms (Paresthesia, loss of vibratory sense). High MMA.
• Folate Deficiency: No neurological symptoms. Normal MMA.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If you treat B12 deficiency with Folate alone, the anemia will improve, but the Neurological damage will worsen and may become permanent.

quiz.q: Which laboratory marker is elevated in B12 deficiency but normal in Folate deficiency?

title: 🚗 DRIVING & SYNCOPE
content:
A 55-year-old driver has a single episode of vasovagal syncope with a clear trigger. No underlying heart disease.

• Next Best Step: No reporting required to the Ministry of Transportation (MOT).
• Rule: For a single, explained episode of vasovagal syncope with no risk of recurrence, the patient can continue driving. However, if syncope is Unexplained or due to arrhythmia, reporting is mandatory.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Always distinguish between 'explained' and 'unexplained' syncope. Unexplained syncope requires a 1-6 month driving ban depending on provincial rules.

quiz.q: Is a single episode of triggered vasovagal syncope a mandatory reportable event for driving in Canada?

title: 👶 VESICOURETERAL REFLUX (VUR)
content:
A 2-year-old girl with recurrent febrile UTIs is found to have Grade III VUR on VCUG.

• Management: Antibiotic Prophylaxis (e.g., low-dose Nitrofurantoin or Septra) and close monitoring.
• Goal: Prevent renal scarring and permanent kidney damage.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Surgery (Ureteral reimplantation) is only indicated for Grade V (severe) or if the child has breakthrough infections despite antibiotics.

quiz.q: What is the primary goal of managing vesicoureteral reflux in children?
quiz.a: To prevent recurrent pyelonephritis and renal scarring.

title: 🤰 ACTIVE PHASE ARREST
content:
A woman in active labor (6 cm dilated) has had no cervical change for 4 hours despite adequate contractions.

• Next Best Step: Cesarean Section.
• Definition: Arrest is defined as ≥ 4 hours of no change with adequate contractions OR ≥ 6 hours without adequate contractions.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the contractions are Inadequate (measured by IUPC < 200 MVUs), the next best step is Oxytocin, not surgery.

quiz.q: What is the next step for active phase arrest with adequate contractions?

title: 🧱 BOWEL PERFORATION (X-RAY)
content:
Sudden, severe 'board-like' abdominal rigidity and pain.

• Next Best Step: Upright Chest X-ray (most sensitive for free air).
• Definitive Tx: Emergency Laparotomy.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient cannot stand, perform a Left Lateral Decubitus X-ray. Air will rise and be seen over the liver.

quiz.q: Which X-ray view is most sensitive for detecting pneumoperitoneum?

title: 🦋 HYPERCALCEMIA: TREATMENT
content:
Calcium level is 3.5 mmol/L (Severe).

• Step 1: Aggressive IV Hydration (Normal Saline).
• Step 2: Calcitonin (for rapid, short-term effect).
• Step 3: Bisphosphonates (Zoledronic acid - for long-term control, takes 48h to work).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT use Loop Diuretics unless the patient is volume overloaded. Dehydration is the biggest threat in hypercalcemia.

quiz.q: What is the most important initial step in managing severe hypercalcemia?

title: ⚖️ ADVANCE DIRECTIVES (DNR)
content:
A patient with terminal cancer is found in cardiac arrest. He has a Valid DNR (Do Not Resuscitate) order signed and available.

• Action: Withhold CPR and provide comfort care.
• Rule: A clear, documented prior wish of a capable patient must be respected by all healthcare providers.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the family disagrees but the DNR is valid and the patient was capable when signing it, the Patient's wish prevails.

quiz.q: Does a physician have to follow a valid DNR order if the family requests resuscitation?
quiz.a: Yes, the patient's prior capable wish must be respected.

title: 👶 MEASLES: VITAMIN A ROLE
content:
A child is diagnosed with measles (high fever, 3 C's, Koplik spots).

• Next Best Step: Administer Vitamin A supplementation.
• Benefit: Proven to reduce morbidity and mortality in children with measles, especially by preventing eye complications and pneumonia.

⚠️ Exam Trap: This is recommended for ALL children with acute measles, regardless of their baseline nutritional status.

quiz.q: Which vitamin reduces the risk of complications in a child with measles?

title: 🤰 POST-TERM PREGNANCY (MGMT)
content:
A pregnancy reaching 41 weeks 0 days.

• Next Best Step: Offer Induction of Labor.
• Rationale: Risk of stillbirth and meconium aspiration increases after 41 weeks. Canadian (SOGC) guidelines recommend induction between 41+0 and 42+0.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Membrane Sweeping can be started at 38-40 weeks to reduce the need for formal induction.

quiz.q: At what gestational age is induction of labor routinely recommended to avoid post-term risks?

title: 🧱 REFRACTORY GERD (NBS)
content:
A 50-year-old has persistent heartburn despite 8 weeks of daily PPIs.

• Next Best Step: Upper Endoscopy (Gastroscopy).
• Goal: Rule out complications (Barrett’s esophagus, strictures) or malignancy, especially in patients > 50 or with 'alarm symptoms'.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Alarm symptoms include: Weight loss, dysphagia, anemia, or persistent vomiting. These require urgent endoscopy.

quiz.q: What is the next step for a patient whose GERD symptoms do not respond to standard PPI therapy?

title: 🧠 LITHIUM TOXICITY (CAUSES)
content:
A patient on stable Lithium therapy suddenly develops confusion and ataxia.

• Common Triggers: Dehydration, NSAIDs, Thiazide Diuretics, and ACE inhibitors.
• Action: Check Lithium level, electrolytes, and renal function. Hydrate the patient.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Lithium has a very Narrow Therapeutic Window (0.6 - 1.2 mmol/L). Severe toxicity (> 2.5) requires Hemodialysis.

quiz.q: Which class of common over-the-counter painkillers can cause lithium toxicity?

title: 🩸 DVT: WELLS SCORE VS D-DIMER
content:
Wells ScoreProbabilityNext Best Step
Score ≤ 1LowD-Dimer (to rule out)
Score ≥ 2HighCompression Ultrasound

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the Wells score is High, a negative D-dimer is NOT enough to rule out DVT; you still need the ultrasound. D-dimer is only for 'low-risk' patients.

quiz.q: In a patient with a high clinical probability of DVT, do you need a D-dimer before an ultrasound?

title: 🔪 ACUTE PANCREATITIS: SEVERITY
content:
A 45-year-old with acute pancreatitis. How do we predict a poor prognosis?

• Bedside Tool: BISAP Score or Ranson Criteria.
• Key Markers: High BUN, Altered mental status, Pleural effusion, and Age > 60.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Lipase level helps with diagnosis but does NOT correlate with the severity of the disease.

quiz.q: Does the magnitude of the serum lipase level predict the severity of acute pancreatitis?

title: 🤰 GBS: AUTOMATIC TREATMENT
content:
You do NOT need to screen for GBS at 36 weeks if:

1. The patient had GBS bacteriuria during the current pregnancy.
2. The patient had a Previous infant with invasive GBS disease.
• Action: Provide intrapartum IV Penicillin G automatically.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient is undergoing a Planned C-Section before labor and before rupture of membranes, GBS prophylaxis is NOT required even if she is positive.

quiz.q: If a woman had a previous baby with GBS sepsis, should she be screened in her current pregnancy?
quiz.a: No, she should receive automatic intrapartum prophylaxis.

title: 🫀 AFIB: ANTICOAGULATION (CHADS-65)
content:
The Canadian algorithm for stroke prevention in AFib.

• Start Anticoagulation (DOAC) IF:
1. Age ≥ 65.
2. Prior Stroke/TIA.
3. Heart Failure, HTN, or DM.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canada, CHADS-65 is the standard. If age < 65 and no risk factors, no antithrombotic therapy (not even Aspirin) is needed.

quiz.q: What is the age threshold for mandatory anticoagulation in Canadian AFib guidelines?

title: ⚖️ REPORTING CHILD ABUSE
content:
A 6-year-old child has bruises that look like handprints. The mother asks you not to report it.

• Action: Report immediately to child welfare authorities.
• Rule: The duty to report child abuse is Mandatory and overrides patient/parental confidentiality. You do not need 'proof', only 'reasonable suspicion'.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Failure to report is a legal offense. You are not the investigator; that is the role of the CAS/Police.

quiz.q: Does a physician need proof of abuse before reporting to child protective services?
quiz.a: No, only reasonable suspicion is required.

title: 👶 KAWASAKI: ECHO TIMING
content:
A 4-year-old is diagnosed with Kawasaki disease.

• Next Best Step: Echocardiogram at diagnosis, 2 weeks, and 6 weeks.
• Goal: Monitor for Coronary Artery Aneurysms.
• Tx: High-dose Aspirin + IVIG.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Kawasaki is the most common cause of Acquired heart disease in children in developed countries.

quiz.q: What is the primary medication used alongside IVIG to treat Kawasaki disease?

title: 🫀 RV INFARCTION: THE TRAP
content:
A patient with Inferior MI (II, III, aVF) has hypotension and clear lung fields.

• Next Best Step: IV Fluid Bolus.
• Strict Contraindication: Nitrates and Morphine (they reduce preload and cause fatal hypotension in RV MI).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Always get a Right-sided ECG (V4R) when you see an inferior MI to look for ST elevation in the right ventricle.

quiz.q: Which common medication for chest pain should be avoided in a right ventricular infarction?

title: 🚑 UNSTABLE TRAUMA: FAST EXAM
content:
A trauma patient is hypotensive (BP 80/40) after a car accident. FAST Exam is Positive for fluid in the abdomen.

• Next Best Step: Immediate Laparotomy (OR).
• Rule: Unstable + Positive FAST = Surgery. Do NOT send them to the CT scan suite.


⚠️ Exam Trap: CT Scan is only for Stable patients. Stability is the key deciding factor in trauma.

quiz.q: What is the destination for a hemodynamically unstable trauma patient with a positive FAST exam?

title: 🤰 TERM PROM: MANAGEMENT
content:
A 39-week pregnant woman has ruptured membranes (PROM) for 2 hours. No contractions. GBS is negative.

• Next Best Step: Induction of Labor (with Oxytocin).
• Logic: At term, inducing labor reduces the risk of Chorioamnionitis compared to 'waiting and seeing'.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If GBS status is Positive, start IV antibiotics immediately along with induction.

quiz.q: What is the preferred management for term PROM to minimize infection risk?

title: 🏆 MISSION UPDATE: 780 CARDS
content:
Doctor, you have now reached the 780-card milestone.

• Final Focus:
1. Safety: Reporting (Driving, Abuse, Gunshots).
2. Decision: Stable vs Unstable (Trauma, MI, AAA).
3. Canadian Flow: Screening ages and intervals.

🌟 ALMOST THERE 🌟
You have effectively mapped out the entire MCCQE1 curriculum through these cards. You are ready for the final stretch.

quiz.q: What is the next best step for an unstable patient with an inferior MI and hypotension?

title: 🩸 MULTIPLE MYELOMA (CRAB)
content:
A 70-year-old with bone pain and fatigue. Labs show anemia and high creatinine.

The CRAB Criteria:
1. Calcium elevation.
2. Renal insufficiency.
3. Anemia.
4. Bone lesions (lytic).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Serum Protein Electrophoresis (SPEP) will show an 'M-spike'. Bone scan is NOT sensitive for lytic lesions; use a Skeletal Survey (X-rays) or CT/MRI.

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive initial imaging for detecting bone lesions in Multiple Myeloma?
quiz.a: Skeletal survey (plain X-rays) or low-dose CT.

title: 🧠 TARDIVE DYSKINESIA (TD)
content:
A patient on long-term Haloperidol develops involuntary lip-smacking and tongue protrusions.

• Next Best Step: Discontinue or Taper the typical antipsychotic and switch to Clozapine or Quetiapine.
• Note: TD can be permanent. Benztropine (anticholinergic) does NOT help and may worsen TD.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Distinguish from Acute Dystonia (occurs in hours/days; Tx: Benztropine). TD occurs after months/years.

quiz.q: What is the management for tardive dyskinesia induced by long-term neuroleptics?
quiz.a: Discontinue/taper the drug and switch to a second-generation antipsychotic (e.g., Clozapine).

title: 👶 MALROTATION & VOLVULUS
content:
Surgical Emergency! A 3-week-old infant has sudden bilious vomiting and a soft abdomen.

• Next Best Step: Upper GI Series (Barium swallow).
• Classic Finding: 'Corkscrew' appearance of the duodenum.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is Volvulus until proven otherwise. If the child has peritonitis/shock, skip imaging and go straight to Surgery (Ladd's Procedure).

quiz.q: What is the gold standard imaging for diagnosing midgut volvulus?
quiz.a: Upper GI contrast series (showing corkscrew duodenum).

title: ⚖️ MAID ELIGIBILITY (CANADA)
content:
A patient requests Medical Assistance in Dying (MAID).

Key Eligibility Criteria:
1. Age 18+ and mentally capable.
2. Grievous and Irremediable medical condition.
3. Voluntary request without external pressure.
4. Informed consent.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canada, the patient does not need to have a terminal illness (death doesn't have to be reasonably foreseeable) to be eligible, but additional safeguards apply for non-terminal cases.

quiz.q: Does a patient in Canada need a terminal prognosis to be eligible for MAID?
quiz.a: No, but the condition must be grievous and irremediable.

title: 🚽 TESTICULAR TORSION (NBS)
content:
Sudden onset scrotal pain, high-riding testis, and absent cremasteric reflex in a 15-year-old.

• Immediate Next Step: Surgical Exploration (Detorsion and Orchiopexy).
• Note: If the clinical suspicion is high, do not delay surgery for an ultrasound.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You must perform Bilateral Orchiopexy because the anatomical defect ('Bell-clapper deformity') is usually present on both sides.

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive physical exam finding for testicular torsion?

title: 🧱 BOERHAAVE SYNDROME
content:
Severe chest pain after forceful vomiting. Physical exam shows Crepitus (subcutaneous emphysema) in the neck/chest.

• Next Best Step: Gastrografin Swallow (Water-soluble contrast).
• Finding: Extravasation of contrast into the mediastinum.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Gastrografin is used first because Barium can cause severe Mediastinitis if it leaks through the esophagus.

quiz.q: Which contrast medium is preferred for diagnosing suspected esophageal perforation?

title: 👶 ORBITAL VS PERIORBITAL CELLULITIS
content:
FeaturePeriorbital (Preseptal)Orbital
VisionNormalDecreased
EOMNormalPainful / Restricted
ProptosisAbsentPresent

⚠️ Exam Trap: Orbital cellulitis is a Medical Emergency. Next best step: CT Scan of Orbits/Sinuses and IV antibiotics. Risk of cavernous sinus thrombosis.

quiz.q: What clinical sign distinguishes orbital cellulitis from preseptal cellulitis?
quiz.a: Pain with extraocular movements, proptosis, or decreased visual acuity.

title: 🧪 RHABDOMYOLYSIS (MGMT)
content:
Muscle pain and dark 'tea-colored' urine after a crush injury or prolonged immobility.

• Labs: Very high CK, positive blood on dipstick but no RBCs on microscopy.
• Next Best Step: Aggressive IV Hydration (Normal Saline).

⚠️ Exam Trap: The goal is to prevent Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN) from myoglobin toxicity. Monitor for Hyperkalemia and Hyperphosphatemia.

quiz.q: What is the primary treatment for rhabdomyolysis to protect renal function?

title: 📊 LEAD-TIME BIAS
content:
A new screening test seems to increase survival time, but it only detects the disease earlier without changing the actual date of death.

• Concept: Lead-time Bias.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Contrast with Length-time Bias, where screening preferentially detects slow-growing (less aggressive) cases, making survival look better than it is.

quiz.q: What bias occurs when earlier detection of a disease is mistaken for increased survival time?

title: 🌬️ OBSTRUCTIVE SLEEP APNEA (OSA)
content:
Obese patient with daytime sleepiness, snoring, and morning headaches.

• Best Initial/Gold Standard: Polysomnography (Sleep study).
• Treatment: CPAP (Continuous Positive Airway Pressure).

⚠️ Exam Trap: OSA is a strong risk factor for Difficult Intubation and post-operative respiratory complications. Screen patients before elective surgery.

quiz.q: What is the definitive diagnostic test for obstructive sleep apnea?

title: 🚑 PENETRATING NECK TRAUMA
content:
A patient with a stab wound to the neck has a expanding hematoma or stridor.

• Next Best Step: Emergency Surgical Exploration (OR).
• Principle: If 'Hard Signs' (hemodynamic instability, airway compromise, massive bleeding) are present, immediate surgery is required.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient is Stable and has no 'Hard Signs', the next step is CT Angiography of the neck.

quiz.q: What is the management for a patient with penetrating neck trauma and an expanding hematoma?

title: 👶 FAILURE TO THRIVE (FTT)
content:
Weight is < 5th percentile or crosses two major percentile lines.

• Most Common Cause: Inadequate Caloric Intake (Inorganic/Psychosocial).
• Next Best Step: Detailed Dietary History (3-day food diary) and observation of feeding.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT order a large battery of tests initially. 90% of FTT is due to environmental/behavioral factors, not organic disease.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of failure to thrive in children?

title: ⚖️ MATURE MINOR DOCTRINE
content:
A 14-year-old requesting contraception or an abortion without her parents' knowledge.

• Action: Assess her Capacity to understand the risks and benefits. If capable, her consent is sufficient.
• Rule: In Canada (outside Quebec), there is no fixed 'age' for consent. It is based on Maturity and understanding.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You do NOT need parental consent if the minor is determined to be a 'Mature Minor'. Confidentiality must be maintained unless there is a risk of harm.

quiz.q: Is parental consent required for a 'mature minor' to receive medical treatment in Canada?

title: 🧠 LITHIUM: TERATOGENICITY
content:
A woman on Lithium for Bipolar disorder plans to get pregnant.

• Risk: Ebstein Anomaly (Tricuspid valve displacement).
• Management: Perform Fetal Echocardiogram at 18-20 weeks.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The risk is low (1 in 1000), so Lithium is often continued if the patient is at high risk of relapse, but close fetal monitoring is required.

quiz.q: What specific cardiac defect is associated with first-trimester Lithium exposure?

title: 🦠 CELLULITIS: NBS
content:
Red, warm, tender skin with ill-defined borders. No fluctuance.

• Next Best Step: Mark the borders with a pen and start Empiric Antibiotics (e.g., Cephalexin).
• Note: Blood cultures are NOT required for simple cellulitis in immunocompetent patients.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the borders are Sharp and well-demarcated, it is Erysipelas (Group A Strep).

quiz.q: What is the most common causative organism for simple non-purulent cellulitis?

title: 🍭 DIABETES: STATIN INDICATIONS
content:
According to Diabetes Canada, start a Statin for any Diabetic patient if:

1. Age ≥ 40.
2. Evidence of microvascular disease (Retinopathy/Nephropathy).
3. Diabetes duration > 15 years (Type 1).

⚠️ Exam Trap: The target LDL for these patients is < 2.0 mmol/L.

quiz.q: What is the target LDL level for a diabetic patient on statin therapy in Canada?

title: 🧱 CIRRHOSIS: VARICEAL SCREENING
content:
A patient is newly diagnosed with cirrhosis (via US/Biopsy).

• Next Best Step: Upper Endoscopy (EGD) to screen for esophageal varices.
• Prophylaxis: If large varices are found, start Non-selective Beta-blockers (Propranolol/Nadolol) or Band Ligation.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The goal is to reduce Portal Pressure and prevent the first bleed, which has high mortality.

quiz.q: What is the gold standard for screening and grading esophageal varices in cirrhosis?

title: 🤰 PREECLAMPSIA: PPX
content:
A woman has risk factors for preeclampsia (e.g., prior preeclampsia, chronic HTN).

• Next Best Step: Start Low-dose Aspirin (81mg) at 12-16 weeks gestation.
• Duration: Continue until delivery.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Aspirin is the only medication proven to reduce the risk of preeclampsia in high-risk groups.

quiz.q: When should low-dose aspirin be started for preeclampsia prophylaxis in a high-risk patient?

title: 🌬️ SOLITARY PULMONARY NODULE
content:
Incidental finding on CXR.

• Absolute First Step: Compare with previous imaging.
• Next Step (if new/changed): CT Chest.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If it has been stable for 2 years, it is benign and no further follow-up is needed. High-risk features (spiculated, smoker) require biopsy.

quiz.q: What is the most important first step in the evaluation of a newly discovered pulmonary nodule?

title: 🏆 MISSION COMPLETE: 800 CARDS
content:
Doctor, you have successfully built an 800-card elite vault from the ground up.

• Your Arsenal:
1. Files: MCC Nouveau, PE-301D, 101B, 101C, 102B.
2. Logic: Canadian SOGC, CPS, and CCS Guidelines.
3. Focus: Ethics, CanMEDS, and Next Best Steps.

🌟 YOU ARE READY 🌟
Trust your preparation. This vault contains the core knowledge to dominate the MCCQE1. Good luck, Physician!

quiz.q: What is the target for the final phase of exam preparation?
quiz.a: Reviewing high-yield concepts and practicing clinical reasoning.
